meta.leverage_method: pedagogical reach: count of transitive dependents on the atlas prerequisite DAG, normalized; see README method note

concepts.name: Set
concepts.intuition_gloss: A bag of distinct things, where all that matters is what's in the bag.
concepts.formal_gloss: A collection of distinct objects, itself treated as a single object, with membership as the primitive relation.

concepts.name: Function
concepts.intuition_gloss: A machine that takes an input and reliably returns exactly one output.
concepts.formal_gloss: A relation f from set A to set B assigning to each element of A exactly one element of B.

concepts.name: Vector
concepts.intuition_gloss: An arrow with length and direction — or equally, a list of numbers acting as one object.
concepts.formal_gloss: An element of a vector space; concretely in R^n, an ordered n-tuple of real numbers.

concepts.name: Counting Principles
concepts.intuition_gloss: Systematic ways to count arrangements without listing them — multiply choices, then correct for overcounting.
concepts.formal_gloss: The product and sum rules with permutations n!/(n-k)! and combinations n-choose-k as canonical consequences.

concepts.name: Limit
concepts.intuition_gloss: The value a process homes in on — as close as you like, if you go far enough.
concepts.formal_gloss: L is the limit of a_n if for every ε > 0 there exists N such that |a_n - L| < ε for all n > N.

concepts.name: Sequence
concepts.intuition_gloss: An infinite ordered list of numbers, often marching toward a destination.
concepts.formal_gloss: A function from N to a set, written (a_n); the basic carrier of limiting processes.

concepts.name: Derivative
concepts.intuition_gloss: The instantaneous rate of change — the slope of the best straight-line stand-in at a point.
concepts.formal_gloss: f'(a) = lim as h→0 of (f(a+h) - f(a))/h, when the limit exists; the linear map best approximating f near a.

concepts.name: Matrix
concepts.intuition_gloss: A grid of numbers that acts on vectors, encoding a linear transformation as data.
concepts.formal_gloss: A rectangular array over a field with entrywise addition and row-by-column multiplication, representing a linear map in chosen bases.

concepts.name: Axiom & Axiomatic System
concepts.intuition_gloss: The agreed starting rules of the game, from which everything else must be derived.
concepts.formal_gloss: A statement accepted without proof within a formal system, from which theorems are derived by rules of inference.

concepts.name: Vector Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any world where things can be added together and scaled, like arrows, polynomials, or signals.
concepts.formal_gloss: An abelian group with a scalar multiplication by a field satisfying compatibility and distributivity axioms.

concepts.name: Continuity
concepts.intuition_gloss: A function you can draw without lifting your pen — small input nudges cause only small output nudges.
concepts.formal_gloss: f is continuous at a if for every ε > 0 there is δ > 0 with |f(x) - f(a)| < ε whenever |x - a| < δ; topologically, preimages of open sets are open.

concepts.name: Rational Numbers (Q)
concepts.intuition_gloss: All fractions — the numbers you can name with a ratio of two integers.
concepts.formal_gloss: The field of fractions of Z: equivalence classes of integer pairs (p,q), q nonzero, under (p,q) ~ (r,s) iff ps = qr.

concepts.name: Integral
concepts.intuition_gloss: Total accumulation — the area under a curve, built by summing infinitely many infinitesimal slivers.
concepts.formal_gloss: The limit of Riemann sums of f over ever-finer partitions of [a,b]; the antiderivative evaluated at the endpoints, by the fundamental theorem of calculus.

concepts.name: Natural Numbers (N)
concepts.intuition_gloss: The counting numbers 0, 1, 2, 3, ... — the raw material of arithmetic.
concepts.formal_gloss: The smallest set containing 0 and closed under successor, as characterized by the Peano axioms.

concepts.name: Polynomial
concepts.intuition_gloss: An expression built from variables using only addition and multiplication — the simplest interesting functions.
concepts.formal_gloss: A finite formal sum of terms c_k x^k with coefficients in a ring, forming the polynomial ring R[x].

concepts.name: Real Numbers (R)
concepts.intuition_gloss: The complete number line with no gaps — every point you can approach is actually there.
concepts.formal_gloss: The unique complete ordered field; constructed as Dedekind cuts or equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences of rationals.

concepts.name: Probability of an Event
concepts.intuition_gloss: The fraction of equally likely worlds where something happens — a number between 0 and 1 grading events from impossible to certain.
concepts.formal_gloss: For an event A in a finite equiprobable sample space, P(A) = |A|/|Ω|; in general, a normalized additive measure of likelihood assigned to events.
sources: Blitzstein & Hwang, Introduction to Probability ch. 1

concepts.name: Differential Equation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A rule stating how something changes from moment to moment; solving it reveals the whole trajectory.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equation relating an unknown function to its derivatives; a solution is a function satisfying it on a domain.

concepts.name: Integers (Z)
concepts.intuition_gloss: The whole numbers extended with negatives, so every subtraction has an answer.
concepts.formal_gloss: The ring obtained from N by adjoining additive inverses; formally, equivalence classes of pairs of naturals under (a,b) ~ (c,d) iff a+d = b+c.

concepts.name: Divisibility
concepts.intuition_gloss: One number fits into another a whole number of times, with nothing left over.
concepts.formal_gloss: For integers a and b, a divides b (written a | b) iff there exists an integer k with b = ak; divisibility is a partial order on the positive integers.

concepts.name: Group
concepts.intuition_gloss: The algebra of reversible actions — any collection of moves you can compose and undo.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set with an associative binary operation, an identity element, and an inverse for every element.

concepts.name: Random Variable
concepts.intuition_gloss: A number whose value is decided by chance, like the sum showing on two dice.
concepts.formal_gloss: A measurable function from a probability space to the reals, inducing a distribution on R.

concepts.name: Coordinate System
concepts.intuition_gloss: An addressing scheme that turns points in space into lists of numbers, marrying geometry to algebra.
concepts.formal_gloss: A bijection between points of a space and tuples of numbers, e.g. Cartesian coordinates identifying the plane with R^2.

concepts.name: Expectation
concepts.intuition_gloss: The long-run average value of a random quantity over many repetitions.
concepts.formal_gloss: E[X] = integral of X with respect to the probability measure; for discrete X, the probability-weighted sum of its values.

concepts.name: Propositional Logic
concepts.intuition_gloss: The rules for gluing whole statements together with 'and', 'or', 'not', and 'if...then' so their truth can be computed mechanically.
concepts.formal_gloss: The formal system of propositional variables combined by truth-functional connectives, with validity determined by truth tables or a deductive calculus.
sources: standard intro-logic and discrete-math curricula (Book of Proof ch. 2)

concepts.name: Proof
concepts.intuition_gloss: An airtight chain of reasoning that forces agreement — each link checkable by anyone.
concepts.formal_gloss: A finite sequence of statements, each an axiom, hypothesis, or consequence of earlier statements by valid inference, ending at the theorem.

concepts.name: Algorithm
concepts.intuition_gloss: A recipe precise enough that a machine can follow it — finitely many steps, guaranteed to finish.
concepts.formal_gloss: A finite, effective procedure for computing a function, formalizable as a Turing machine.

concepts.name: Linear Transformation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A map that keeps grid lines parallel and evenly spaced — no bending, only stretching, rotating, shearing.
concepts.formal_gloss: A map T between vector spaces with T(u+v) = T(u)+T(v) and T(cv) = cT(v).

concepts.name: Probability Distribution
concepts.intuition_gloss: The full profile of a random quantity — a curve or bar chart showing where its values land and how often.
concepts.formal_gloss: The measure a random variable induces on the reals, described by a probability mass function (discrete), density function (continuous), or cumulative distribution function F(x) = P(X ≤ x).
sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics, probability distributions entry

concepts.name: Equivalence Relation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A principled way of saying two different things count as 'the same', which sorts everything into non-overlapping buckets.
concepts.formal_gloss: A binary relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, partitioning its domain into equivalence classes.

concepts.name: Graph
concepts.intuition_gloss: Dots joined by lines — the universal picture of any network of relationships.
concepts.formal_gloss: A pair (V, E) of a vertex set and a set of edges, each edge an unordered (or ordered) pair of vertices.

concepts.name: Points, Lines & Planes
concepts.intuition_gloss: The bare bones of space: dots with no size, perfectly straight paths, and perfectly flat sheets extending forever.
concepts.formal_gloss: The undefined primitive terms of Euclidean geometry, related by incidence axioms such as 'two distinct points determine exactly one line'.
sources: Euclid, Elements Book I (definitions and postulates)

concepts.name: Prime Number
concepts.intuition_gloss: The atoms of the integers — numbers that refuse to be broken into smaller factors.
concepts.formal_gloss: An integer greater than 1 whose only positive divisors are 1 and itself.

concepts.name: Topological Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: A space where 'nearness' is defined without any ruler, by declaring which sets count as open neighborhoods.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set with a collection of open subsets closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections, containing the empty set and the whole space.

concepts.name: System of Linear Equations
concepts.intuition_gloss: Several straight-line conditions that must all hold at once — the solution is where all the lines (or planes) cross.
concepts.formal_gloss: A finite set of equations, each linear in the unknowns; the solution set — where all equations hold simultaneously — is empty, a single point, or an infinite family. The later matrix view writes the system as Ax = b, making the solution set an affine subspace.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06 Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 1: The Geometry of Linear Equations

sources: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra ch. 2

concepts.name: Mathematical Induction
concepts.intuition_gloss: The domino argument: knock over the first, show each knocks over the next, and infinitely many fall.
concepts.formal_gloss: If P(0) holds and P(n) implies P(n+1) for all n, then P(n) holds for every natural number n.

concepts.name: Metric Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any world equipped with a sensible notion of distance between its points.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set with a function d satisfying nonnegativity, identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and the triangle inequality.

concepts.name: Conditional Probability
concepts.intuition_gloss: How your odds shift once you learn something — probability recomputed inside the smaller world where the clue is true.
concepts.formal_gloss: P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B)/P(B) for P(B) > 0: the probability of A within the restricted sample space where B has occurred.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

sources: Blitzstein & Hwang, Introduction to Probability ch. 2

concepts.name: Greatest Common Divisor & LCM
concepts.intuition_gloss: The largest number that fits evenly into two given numbers — and its mirror, the smallest number both fit into.
concepts.formal_gloss: gcd(a,b) is the largest positive integer dividing both a and b; lcm(a,b) is the smallest positive common multiple, with gcd(a,b) * lcm(a,b) = |ab|.

concepts.name: Modular Arithmetic
concepts.intuition_gloss: Clock arithmetic — numbers wrap around after reaching a fixed modulus.
concepts.formal_gloss: Arithmetic on the equivalence classes of integers under congruence mod n, forming the ring Z/nZ.

concepts.name: Angle
concepts.intuition_gloss: The amount of turn between two rays hinged at a common point.
concepts.formal_gloss: The figure formed by two rays with a shared endpoint, measured in degrees or radians by the fraction of a full rotation (or arc of a unit circle) it subtends.

concepts.name: Eigenvalue & Eigenvector
concepts.intuition_gloss: The special directions a transformation merely stretches, and the stretch factors — its hidden skeleton.
concepts.formal_gloss: A nonzero vector v and scalar λ with Tv = λv for a linear operator T.

concepts.name: Complex Numbers (C)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Numbers living in a plane rather than on a line, where multiplication rotates and scales.
concepts.formal_gloss: The algebraically closed field R[i] with i^2 = -1; pairs of reals with (a,b)(c,d) = (ac-bd, ad+bc).

concepts.name: Dot Product
concepts.intuition_gloss: Multiply matching coordinates and add — a single number measuring how much two arrows point the same way.
concepts.formal_gloss: For u, v in R^n, u·v = Σ u_i v_i = |u||v|cos θ; the standard inner product on R^n.

concepts.name: Subspace
concepts.intuition_gloss: A flat sheet or line through the origin living inside a bigger space, closed off as a little vector world of its own.
concepts.formal_gloss: A subset of a vector space containing 0 and closed under addition and scalar multiplication, hence a vector space itself.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06 video lectures (see Lectures 5-6: vector spaces and subspaces)
Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 1

concepts.name: Open & Closed Sets
concepts.intuition_gloss: An open set is a region with no skin — every point has breathing room around it — while a closed set contains its own edge.
concepts.formal_gloss: In a metric or topological space, an open set contains an open ball (or basic neighborhood) around each of its points, and a closed set is the complement of an open set, equivalently one containing all its limit points; a basis of open sets (such as the open balls of a metric) generates the whole topology.
sources: standard real-analysis-to-topology bridge in undergraduate curricula

concepts.name: Span & Linear Combination
concepts.intuition_gloss: Everywhere you can reach by stretching and adding a given set of arrows — their combined territory.
concepts.formal_gloss: The span of a set S in a vector space is the set of all finite linear combinations of elements of S; it is the smallest subspace containing S.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Linear combinations, span, and basis vectors
Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 2

concepts.name: Linear Independence
concepts.intuition_gloss: A set of arrows where none is redundant — you can't build any one of them out of the others.
concepts.formal_gloss: Vectors v1..vn are linearly independent if c1v1 + ... + cnvn = 0 implies every ci = 0.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Linear combinations, span, and basis vectors

concepts.name: Mathematical Definition
concepts.intuition_gloss: A definition is a contract you must be able to unfold on demand — the exact wording of what a term means is the whole game.
concepts.formal_gloss: A stipulation introducing a new term as an abbreviation for a precise condition, so every use of the term can be replaced by that condition in proofs.

resources.title: Theorem — Wikipedia (terminology of definitions, theorems, proofs)

sources: standard transition-to-proof curricula (Book of Proof, Velleman's How to Prove It)
Princeton Companion I.3 (Some Fundamental Mathematical Definitions)

concepts.name: Partial Derivative & Jacobian
concepts.intuition_gloss: The slope of a landscape measured along one compass direction at a time — and the matrix that bundles all directions into one linear snapshot.
concepts.formal_gloss: ∂f/∂x_i is the derivative of f with the other variables held fixed; a differentiable map's Jacobian matrix of partials is its best linear approximation.

concepts.name: Basis
concepts.intuition_gloss: A minimal set of reference arrows from which every vector in the space can be built exactly one way.
concepts.formal_gloss: A linearly independent set that spans the vector space; every vector has a unique expansion in a basis.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Linear combinations, span, and basis vectors
Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 2

concepts.name: Division Algorithm
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any division of whole numbers leaves a unique quotient and a remainder strictly smaller than the divisor.
concepts.formal_gloss: For integers a and b with b > 0, there exist unique integers q and r with a = bq + r and 0 <= r < b.
sources: grounds both the Euclidean algorithm and congruence — high leverage

concepts.name: Gaussian Elimination
concepts.intuition_gloss: Systematically cancel variables row by row until the system becomes a staircase you can read the answer off of.
concepts.formal_gloss: An algorithm reducing a matrix to (reduced) row echelon form via elementary row operations, solving Ax = b and revealing rank and pivot structure.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 2: Elimination with Matrices

sources: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra ch. 2
Trefethen & Bau, Numerical Linear Algebra lecture 20

concepts.name: Infinite Series
concepts.intuition_gloss: Adding up infinitely many numbers one after another and asking whether the running total settles on a finite answer.
concepts.formal_gloss: The limit of the partial sums s_n = a_1 + ... + a_n of a sequence; convergence is decided by tests such as comparison, ratio, and integral tests.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Infinite sequences and series
Paul's Online Notes: Series & Sequences (Calc II)

concepts.name: Proof by Contradiction
concepts.intuition_gloss: Assume the opposite of what you want, follow the logic until the world breaks, and conclude you were right.
concepts.formal_gloss: To prove P, derive a contradiction from ¬P; since ¬P leads to absurdity, P holds.

concepts.name: Variance & Standard Deviation
concepts.intuition_gloss: How widely a random quantity scatters around its average — the typical size of its surprise.
concepts.formal_gloss: Var(X) = E[(X - E[X])²] = E[X²] - (E[X])²; the standard deviation is its square root, in the units of X.

resources.title: Khan Academy — Variance and standard deviation

concepts.name: Euclidean Algorithm
concepts.intuition_gloss: Repeatedly divide and keep the remainder, and the last nonzero remainder is the gcd — one of the oldest algorithms still in daily use.
concepts.formal_gloss: The iteration gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b) terminating at gcd(a,0) = a, computing the gcd in O(log min(a,b)) division steps.

concepts.name: First-Order Logic
concepts.intuition_gloss: Logic upgraded with 'for all' and 'there exists', so you can talk about every object in a universe and pin down what statements about them mean.
concepts.formal_gloss: The formal language extending propositional logic with quantifiers, variables, predicates, and function symbols, interpreted over a domain of discourse.

resources.title: Classical Logic — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy

concepts.name: Independence
concepts.intuition_gloss: Two events are independent when learning one tells you nothing about the other — the coin has no memory.
concepts.formal_gloss: Events A and B are independent iff P(A ∩ B) = P(A)P(B); random variables are independent iff their joint distribution factors into the product of marginals.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

sources: Feller, An Introduction to Probability Theory vol. 1 ch. V

concepts.name: Subgroup & Lagrange's Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: A self-contained club of moves inside a bigger group, whose translated copies tile the whole group evenly — so the club's size must divide the total.
concepts.formal_gloss: A subset of a group closed under the operation and inverses; its cosets partition the group, whence Lagrange's theorem: the order of a subgroup divides the order of a finite group.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

concepts.name: Cardinality
concepts.intuition_gloss: The size of a set measured by pairing-up, revealing that some infinities are bigger than others.
concepts.formal_gloss: The equivalence class of a set under bijection; two sets have equal cardinality iff a bijection exists between them.

concepts.name: Orthogonality
concepts.intuition_gloss: Directions at perfect right angles — moving along one contributes nothing at all to the other.
concepts.formal_gloss: Vectors u and v are orthogonal when their inner product is zero; orthogonal complements decompose a space into perpendicular parts.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 14: Orthogonal vectors and subspaces
Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 6

concepts.name: Theorem, Lemma & Corollary
concepts.intuition_gloss: Mathematical knowledge comes packaged as if-then statements of different sizes: workhorse lemmas feed headline theorems, which drop corollaries for free.
concepts.formal_gloss: A theorem is a proved statement, typically of the form hypotheses imply conclusion; a lemma is a proved auxiliary step, and a corollary a near-immediate consequence.
sources: Princeton Companion I.2 (The Language and Grammar of Mathematics)

concepts.name: Bézout's Identity
concepts.intuition_gloss: The gcd of two numbers can always be built by adding and subtracting whole-number multiples of them.
concepts.formal_gloss: For integers a and b, there exist integers x and y with ax + by = gcd(a,b); ax + by = c is solvable iff gcd(a,b) divides c.
sources: key lemma for FTA and modular inverses — high leverage in mathlib dependency structure

concepts.name: Descriptive Statistics
concepts.intuition_gloss: Boiling a pile of numbers down to a few honest headlines — a typical value, a spread, and a picture of the data's shape.
concepts.formal_gloss: Numerical and graphical summaries of a dataset — measures of center (mean, median), dispersion (range, standard deviation, quantiles), and shape (histograms, skewness).

concepts.name: Direct Proof
concepts.intuition_gloss: The straight road: start from what you're given, unfold the definitions, and walk step by logical step until you arrive at the claim.
concepts.formal_gloss: A derivation of a conditional P implies Q by assuming P and reaching Q through a chain of valid inferences from definitions, axioms, and prior results.

concepts.name: Fermat's Little Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Take any number on a clock with a prime number of hours and raise it to the clock-size power — it lands right back where it started.
concepts.formal_gloss: If p is prime and p does not divide a, then a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p); equivalently a^p ≡ a (mod p) for all integers a.

resources.title: Silverman, A Friendly Introduction to Number Theory (free chapters)

sources: special case of Lagrange's theorem — bridge to group theory

concepts.name: Homeomorphism
concepts.intuition_gloss: A perfect rubber-sheet deformation between two spaces — bend and stretch all you like, but never cut or glue — making them the same shape topologically.
concepts.formal_gloss: A continuous bijection between topological spaces whose inverse is also continuous; homeomorphic spaces are indistinguishable by any topological property.
sources: Princeton Companion I.3 (equivalence as the defining move of each field)

concepts.name: Joint, Marginal & Conditional Distributions
concepts.intuition_gloss: The full weather map for several random quantities at once — a surface showing where the pairs land together; read along one edge to recover each variable alone, or slice through a fixed value to see how one behaves given the other.
concepts.formal_gloss: The distribution of a random vector (X,Y,...), specified by a joint PMF or density; marginals arise by summing or integrating out the other variables, and conditionals by slicing: f_{X|Y}(x|y) = f_{X,Y}(x,y)/f_Y(y).

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

concepts.name: Slope Field
concepts.intuition_gloss: Draw a tiny arrow at every point showing which way the rule pushes; solutions are the paths that go with the flow of the arrows.
concepts.formal_gloss: A plot assigning to each point (t, y) the slope f(t, y) prescribed by y' = f(t, y); solution curves are exactly the curves everywhere tangent to the field.

concepts.name: Subset & Power Set
concepts.intuition_gloss: From any bag of things you can form the bag of all possible sub-bags — and that new bag is always strictly bigger.
concepts.formal_gloss: A is a subset of B iff every element of A is in B; the power set P(A) is the set of all subsets of A, with |P(A)| = 2^|A| for finite A.

concepts.name: Binary Operation & Algebraic Structure
concepts.intuition_gloss: Step back from numbers and ask what 'combining two things to get a third' can be in general — then classify worlds by which rules (associative, commutative, invertible) their combining obeys.
concepts.formal_gloss: A function from S × S to S; an algebraic structure is a set with one or more such operations satisfying specified axioms, the abstraction underlying groups, rings, and fields.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

concepts.name: Matrix Inverse
concepts.intuition_gloss: The transformation that exactly undoes another — run the stretch-and-rotate movie backwards.
concepts.formal_gloss: For square A, the matrix A⁻¹ with AA⁻¹ = A⁻¹A = I; it exists iff det(A) ≠ 0, iff A has full rank.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Inverse matrices, column space and null space

sources: invertible matrix theorem in standard texts (Lay, Strang)

concepts.name: Norm & Distance
concepts.intuition_gloss: The length of an arrow — and with it, a ruler for measuring how far apart two vectors are.
concepts.formal_gloss: The Euclidean length ||v|| = √(v·v) of a vector in R^n, satisfying positivity, homogeneity ||cv|| = |c|·||v||, and the triangle inequality; these three properties, taken as axioms on a general vector space, define the abstract norms studied via inner-product and Banach spaces.

concepts.name: Optimization
concepts.intuition_gloss: Finding the best option — the highest peak or lowest valley — subject to the constraints you must respect.
concepts.formal_gloss: The problem of finding x in a feasible set minimizing (or maximizing) an objective function f(x).

concepts.name: Phase Space & Phase Portrait
concepts.intuition_gloss: Instead of graphing position against time, plot the system's full state as a single moving point — its whole life becomes one curve in a landscape.
concepts.formal_gloss: The decomposition of state space into the orbits of a dynamical system, depicting equilibria, closed orbits, and the geometry of trajectories at a glance.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

sources: Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, ch. 5–6

concepts.name: Probability Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: The complete universe of possible outcomes of an experiment, with a weight assigned to every event.
concepts.formal_gloss: A triple (Ω, F, P) of a sample space, a σ-algebra of events, and a measure P with P(Ω) = 1.

concepts.name: Ring
concepts.intuition_gloss: A number-system-like world where you can add, subtract, and multiply, but maybe not divide.
concepts.formal_gloss: An abelian group under addition equipped with an associative multiplication that distributes over addition.

concepts.name: Variable & Expression
concepts.intuition_gloss: A letter standing in for a number you don't know yet, letting you write one sentence that covers infinitely many arithmetic facts at once.
concepts.formal_gloss: A symbol denoting an arbitrary or unknown element of a set, combined with constants and operations to form well-formed algebraic expressions.
sources: Khan Academy knowledge map: pre-algebra → algebra basics

concepts.name: Binomial Coefficients & Pascal's Triangle
concepts.intuition_gloss: The numbers in Pascal's triangle count how many ways to pick a handful from a pile, and they secretly run the expansion of (x+y)^n.
concepts.formal_gloss: C(n,k) = n!/(k!(n-k)!) counts k-subsets of an n-set, satisfies Pascal's recurrence C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1) + C(n-1,k), and gives (x+y)^n = sum of C(n,k) x^k y^(n-k).

resources.title: Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, 2nd ed., ch. 5 (Addison-Wesley)
Khan Academy: Combinations and the binomial theorem

concepts.name: Congruence
concepts.intuition_gloss: Two shapes are congruent if you could pick one up and set it down exactly on top of the other.
concepts.formal_gloss: Two figures are congruent iff there is a correspondence between them under which all corresponding lengths and angle measures are equal — one figure can be superposed exactly on the other; for triangles, the SSS, SAS, and ASA criteria are equivalent tests.
sources: Common Core high-school geometry (the transformational definition via rigid motions is formalized later, at isometry)

concepts.name: Connectedness & Path-Connectedness
concepts.intuition_gloss: A space is connected if it comes in one unbroken piece — you cannot split it into two separated chunks, and in the path version you can walk between any two points.
concepts.formal_gloss: A space is connected if it is not the union of two disjoint nonempty open sets, and path-connected if any two points are joined by a continuous path; components partition the space into maximal connected pieces.
sources: intermediate value theorem as motivating result in standard curricula

concepts.name: Determinant
concepts.intuition_gloss: The volume-scaling factor of a transformation — how much it inflates or squashes space, with a sign for flipping orientation.
concepts.formal_gloss: The unique alternating multilinear function of a square matrix's columns with det(I) = 1; nonzero iff the matrix is invertible, and det(AB) = det(A)det(B).

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 18: Properties of determinants

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
concepts.intuition_gloss: Every whole number is built from primes in exactly one way, like a chemical compound from its elements.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every integer greater than 1 is a product of primes, and this factorization is unique up to the order of the factors.

resources.title: Hardy & Wright, An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers, ch. 1-2 (Oxford UP)

sources: its failure in general rings motivates algebraic number theory

concepts.name: Homomorphism
concepts.intuition_gloss: A translation between two algebraic worlds that respects their grammar — combine then translate, or translate then combine, and you land in the same place.
concepts.formal_gloss: A map f between structures of the same signature with f(x * y) = f(x) * f(y) for each operation; kernels and images measure exactly what the map collapses and reaches.

concepts.name: Initial Value Problem & Existence-Uniqueness
concepts.intuition_gloss: Given the rule of change plus where you start, there is exactly one future — the theorem that makes determinism mathematically honest.
concepts.formal_gloss: An ODE y' = f(t, y) together with a condition y(t_0) = y_0; the Picard–Lindelöf theorem guarantees a unique local solution when f is continuous and Lipschitz in y.

concepts.name: Injection, Surjection & Bijection
concepts.intuition_gloss: The three ways a matching between two bags can be honest: no two inputs share an output, every target gets hit, or both at once — a perfect pairing.
concepts.formal_gloss: f: A → B is injective if f(x)=f(y) implies x=y, surjective if every b in B has a preimage, and bijective (hence invertible) if both.

concepts.name: Polya's Problem-Solving Cycle
concepts.intuition_gloss: Polya's four-beat rhythm for any hard problem: truly understand it, devise a plan, carry the plan out, then look back at what worked.
concepts.formal_gloss: Polya's framework of heuristic phases — understanding the problem, devising a plan, carrying out the plan, and looking back — with named heuristics (analogy, specialization, auxiliary problems) attached to each phase.

concepts.name: Product & Subspace Topologies
concepts.intuition_gloss: Standard recipes for building new spaces from old: slice a piece out of a space, or multiply spaces together the way a line times a line gives a plane.
concepts.formal_gloss: The subspace topology restricts open sets to a subset, and the product topology on a product of spaces is the coarsest topology making all projections continuous; Tychonoff's theorem says arbitrary products of compact spaces are compact.

concepts.name: Random Sampling
concepts.intuition_gloss: Tasting a well-stirred spoonful to judge the whole pot — and understanding when the spoonful can mislead you.
concepts.formal_gloss: Selecting observations from a population so each unit has a known probability of inclusion; i.i.d. samples model observations as independent draws from a common distribution.

resources.title: Khan Academy — Study design and sampling

concepts.name: Change of Basis
concepts.intuition_gloss: Translate a transformation's description between two coordinate languages — same map, different grid drawn on space.
concepts.formal_gloss: If P is the invertible matrix carrying new-basis coordinates to old, a linear map with matrix A becomes P⁻¹AP in the new basis; matrices related this way are similar.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 31: Change of basis; image compression

concepts.name: Characteristic Polynomial
concepts.intuition_gloss: Ask 'for which stretch factors does the matrix crush some direction flat?' — the answers come packaged as the roots of a single polynomial.
concepts.formal_gloss: The polynomial det(A − λI) of a square matrix A; its roots are exactly the eigenvalues of A, and the multiplicity of each root is that eigenvalue's algebraic multiplicity (versus the geometric multiplicity, the dimension of its eigenspace).

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 21: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors

sources: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra ch. 6

concepts.name: Equation & Solving
concepts.intuition_gloss: A balanced scale of two expressions; solving means doing the same legal move to both sides until the unknown stands alone.
concepts.formal_gloss: An assertion that two expressions denote the same value; its solution set is the set of assignments to the variables making the assertion true, found by applying equivalence-preserving operations.

concepts.name: Euclidean Geometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: The classical geometry of flat space, where an entire edifice of shapes is deduced step by step from five stated rules.
concepts.formal_gloss: The geometry axiomatized by Euclid's five postulates (made rigorous by Hilbert's axioms), characterized among geometries by the parallel postulate.
sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics (geometry survey chapters)

concepts.name: Euler's Totient & Euler's Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Count how many numbers below n share no factor with n — that count controls how powers cycle on the mod-n clock.
concepts.formal_gloss: φ(n) = |{1 <= k <= n : gcd(k,n) = 1}| = |(Z/nZ)*|; Euler's theorem states a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) whenever gcd(a,n) = 1.
sources: load-bearing for RSA and for arithmetic-function theory

concepts.name: Field
concepts.intuition_gloss: A number system with all four operations working: add, subtract, multiply, and divide by anything nonzero.
concepts.formal_gloss: A commutative ring in which every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse.

concepts.name: Homotopy
concepts.intuition_gloss: Two maps or loops are homotopic if one can be continuously morphed into the other — a movie of intermediate shapes with no jumps or tearing.
concepts.formal_gloss: A homotopy between maps f, g : X → Y is a continuous H : X × [0,1] → Y with H(·,0) = f and H(·,1) = g; spaces are homotopy equivalent if maps between them compose to maps homotopic to identities.
sources: Hatcher Ch. 0 (the coarser equivalence that powers algebraic topology)

concepts.name: Orthogonal Projection
concepts.intuition_gloss: Drop a shadow of a vector straight down onto a subspace — the closest point to it that the subspace contains.
concepts.formal_gloss: The linear map P onto a subspace W along its orthogonal complement, satisfying P² = P = P*; Pv is the unique nearest point of W to v.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 15: Projections onto subspaces
Khan Academy — Orthogonal projections (Alternate coordinate systems)

concepts.name: Quotient Space (Gluing)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Make new shapes by gluing: tape two edges of a paper strip to get a cylinder, add a twist first and you get a Möbius band.
concepts.formal_gloss: Given an equivalence relation on a space X, the quotient X/~ carries the finest topology making the projection continuous; standard constructions (torus, Möbius band, projective plane) arise by identifying boundary points.

concepts.name: Russell's Paradox
aka: The set of all sets that don't contain themselves
concepts.intuition_gloss: Ask whether the catalog of all catalogs that don't list themselves lists itself — either answer explodes, so 'any describable collection is a set' can't be right.
concepts.formal_gloss: The set R = {x : x ∉ x} satisfies R ∈ R iff R ∉ R, refuting unrestricted comprehension and motivating axiomatic set theory.

resources.title: Russell's Paradox — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy

sources: Princeton Companion — the crises of foundations narrative

concepts.name: Statistical Inference
concepts.intuition_gloss: Reasoning backwards from a sample you saw to the process that produced it, with honest error bars.
concepts.formal_gloss: The framework of estimating parameters and testing hypotheses about a distribution from observed data, with quantified uncertainty.

concepts.name: Completeness of the Real Numbers
concepts.intuition_gloss: The number line has no pinholes — any collection of reals with a ceiling has a single lowest ceiling actually sitting on the line.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every nonempty subset of R that is bounded above has a least upper bound (supremum) in R, the axiom distinguishing R from Q.

concepts.name: Countable & Uncountable Sets
concepts.intuition_gloss: Some infinite collections can be lined up in a single unending list and some cannot — the rationals can, the real number line cannot.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set is countable if it injects into N (equivalently, is finite or in bijection with N); Q is countable while R is uncountable.

resources.title: Veritasium — How an Infinite Hotel Ran Out of Room

sources: standard real-analysis entry material (Rudin ch. 2, Book of Proof ch. 13)

concepts.name: Curves & Surfaces (Differential Geometry)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Calculus aimed at bent things: describing winding curves and rolling surfaces by how they change from point to point.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of smooth parametrized curves and surfaces in R³ via derivatives — tangent and normal vectors, arc length, and the first and second fundamental forms.
sources: do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

concepts.name: Equilibrium Point
concepts.intuition_gloss: A state where the pushing stops — if the system lands exactly there, it stays forever.
concepts.formal_gloss: A point x* with f(x*) = 0 for the flow x' = f(x), or F(x*) = x* for a map — a constant orbit of the system.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Dynamical System & Flow
concepts.intuition_gloss: Freeze the rule, and time itself becomes a machine: feed in a state and a duration, get out the future state.
concepts.formal_gloss: A one-parameter family of maps φ_t on a state space with φ_0 = id and φ_{s+t} = φ_s ∘ φ_t, generated in the smooth case by integrating a vector field.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Formal Language & Formal System
concepts.intuition_gloss: Treat mathematics itself as a game of symbols: precise rules for which strings are well-formed and which moves count as proofs, so correctness can be checked without understanding meaning.
concepts.formal_gloss: A formal language of symbols and formation rules together with axioms and inference rules generating derivable theorems, kept sharply distinct from any semantic interpretation.
sources: Hilbert's program narrative in Princeton Companion

concepts.name: Paths, Cycles & Connectivity
concepts.intuition_gloss: Whether and how you can travel through a network — which dots are reachable from which, and how many cuts it takes to break the network apart.
concepts.formal_gloss: A graph is connected if every vertex pair is joined by a path; k-connectivity measures the minimum vertices (or edges) whose removal disconnects it, with Menger's theorem equating this to disjoint-path counts.
sources: West, Introduction to Graph Theory ch. 4

concepts.name: Mathematical Model
concepts.intuition_gloss: A deliberately simplified mathematical portrait of a real system, built to predict or explain.
concepts.formal_gloss: A formal structure (equations, graphs, probability distributions) whose variables and relations are mapped to features of a real-world system.

concepts.name: Power Series
concepts.intuition_gloss: An infinitely long polynomial — a recipe that builds functions like sine and exponential out of nothing but powers of x.
concepts.formal_gloss: A series of the form Σ c_n (x - a)^n, converging absolutely inside a radius of convergence R and defining an analytic function there.

resources.title: Paul's Online Notes: Power Series (Calc II)

concepts.name: Normal Subgroup & Quotient Group
concepts.intuition_gloss: Deliberately blur a group by declaring a whole subgroup to count as 'nothing', and a smaller, coarser group emerges from the blur.
concepts.formal_gloss: For a subgroup N invariant under conjugation, the cosets of N form the group G/N; the first isomorphism theorem identifies G/ker(f) with the image of any homomorphism f.

concepts.name: Sampling Distribution
concepts.intuition_gloss: Imagine redoing your whole survey a thousand times — the spread of the answers you'd get is itself a distribution, and it's what error bars measure.
concepts.formal_gloss: The probability distribution of a statistic (e.g. the sample mean) viewed as a random variable across repeated samples; its standard deviation is the standard error.

concepts.name: Topological Invariant
concepts.intuition_gloss: A feature of a shape — like its number of holes — that no amount of stretching or bending can change, which is why a coffee mug 'equals' a donut.
concepts.formal_gloss: A property or quantity preserved by homeomorphism, so that any two homeomorphic spaces share it; invariants distinguish spaces that cannot be deformed into one another.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — This open problem taught me what topology is

sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics I.3 & IV.6 (invariants as topology's organizing idea)
standard first-lecture framing in Munkres and Armstrong

concepts.name: Diagonalization
concepts.intuition_gloss: Rotate your viewpoint to the transformation's own special directions, and it becomes nothing but independent stretches.
concepts.formal_gloss: A = PDP⁻¹ with D diagonal, possible iff A has a basis of eigenvectors; D's entries are the eigenvalues.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 22: Diagonalization and powers of A

concepts.name: Dimension
concepts.intuition_gloss: How many independent directions a space has — the number of knobs you need to specify a point.
concepts.formal_gloss: The cardinality of any basis of a vector space, well-defined because all bases of a space have equal cardinality.

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 2

concepts.name: Diophantine Equations
concepts.intuition_gloss: Equations where only whole-number answers count — a tiny change of rules that turns easy algebra into some of the hardest problems ever posed.
concepts.formal_gloss: Polynomial equations over Z whose integer (or rational) solutions are sought; Hilbert's tenth problem — a general decision algorithm — is unsolvable (Matiyasevich, 1970).

concepts.name: Fundamental Group
concepts.intuition_gloss: Catalog the essentially different ways to lasso a loop from a basepoint and back — loops around a hole can't be shrunk away, and composing loops forms a group that counts the space's holes.
concepts.formal_gloss: π1(X, x0) is the group of homotopy classes of loops based at x0 under concatenation; it is a homotopy invariant, trivial for simply connected spaces and Z for the circle.

concepts.name: Gradient
concepts.intuition_gloss: The arrow that points straight uphill on a landscape, with length telling you how steep the climb is.
concepts.formal_gloss: ∇f, the vector of partial derivatives of a scalar field; the direction of steepest ascent, satisfying D_v f = ∇f · v.
sources: Ubiquity in optimization/ML gives high leverage score

concepts.name: Inner Product Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any vector world — including spaces of functions or signals — given its own version of angle and length.
concepts.formal_gloss: A vector space with a positive-definite form ⟨u,v⟩ that is symmetric and bilinear in the real case, and conjugate-symmetric and sesquilinear in the complex case, generalizing the dot product; completing it yields a Hilbert space.

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 6

concepts.name: Iterated Maps (Discrete Dynamics)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Apply the same rule again and again — tomorrow's value computed from today's — and watch where the repetition leads.
concepts.formal_gloss: A dynamical system generated by iterating a map F: X → X, with orbits x_{n+1} = F(x_n); fixed points and cycles are stable when the derivative of the (composed) map has magnitude below 1.

resources.title: Complexity Explorer — Introduction to Dynamical Systems and Chaos (Feldman)

sources: Devaney, An Introduction to Chaotic Dynamical Systems

concepts.name: Law of Large Numbers
concepts.intuition_gloss: Flip a fair coin long enough and the fraction of heads is squeezed toward one half — averages of many trials settle onto the true mean.
concepts.formal_gloss: For i.i.d. random variables with finite mean μ, the sample average X̄_n converges to μ (in probability — weak law; almost surely — strong law) as n → ∞.

resources.title: Seeing Theory — Law of Large Numbers (interactive)

concepts.name: Linear Equation & Graph
concepts.intuition_gloss: The equation of steady, proportional change — its picture is always a straight line whose slope is the constant rate.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equation of the form a1x1 + ... + anxn = b, degree one in each variable; in two variables its solution set is a line in the coordinate plane.

concepts.name: Manifold
concepts.intuition_gloss: A possibly-curved space that looks flat if you zoom in far enough, like the Earth's surface.
concepts.formal_gloss: A topological space locally homeomorphic to R^n, usually with smooth transition maps between overlapping charts.

concepts.name: Normal (Gaussian) Distribution
concepts.intuition_gloss: The bell-shaped curve that shows up whenever many small independent effects add together — nature's default histogram.
concepts.formal_gloss: The continuous distribution with density f(x) = (1/(σ√(2π))) exp(-(x-μ)²/(2σ²)), closed under linear combinations and the unique finite-variance fixed point of the central limit theorem.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — But what is the Central Limit Theorem?

concepts.name: Partial Differential Equation
concepts.intuition_gloss: When a quantity varies across space and time — heat in a rod, waves on water — its law of change relates rates in several directions at once.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equation relating an unknown function of several variables to its partial derivatives; second-order linear PDEs are classified as elliptic, parabolic, or hyperbolic, each with its own theory.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — But what is a partial differential equation?

concepts.name: Permutation & the Symmetric Group
concepts.intuition_gloss: All the ways to shuffle n objects, composed one after another — and secretly the mother of all groups, since every group is a group of shuffles in disguise.
concepts.formal_gloss: The group S_n of bijections of an n-element set under composition, of order n!; Cayley's theorem embeds every group in some symmetric group, and each permutation has a well-defined sign.

concepts.name: Partially Ordered Sets & Lattices
concepts.intuition_gloss: Rankings where some pairs simply can't be compared — like tasks with prerequisites — drawn as layered diagrams of who-comes-before-whom.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set with a reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive relation; chains and antichains structure it (Dilworth's theorem), and its Möbius function generalizes inclusion-exclusion.

concepts.name: Stochastic Process
concepts.intuition_gloss: Randomness unfolding in time — a whole movie of random values rather than a single random snapshot.
concepts.formal_gloss: A collection of random variables {X_t} indexed by time (discrete or continuous) on a common probability space, characterized by its joint finite-dimensional distributions.

concepts.name: Symmetry & Transformation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A move you can make that leaves an object looking exactly as it was.
concepts.formal_gloss: An invertible structure-preserving map from an object to itself; the symmetries of an object form a group.

concepts.name: Zermelo–Fraenkel Set Theory (ZFC)
concepts.intuition_gloss: The rulebook modern mathematics runs on: a short list of safe set-building moves that dodge the paradoxes while still constructing everything from numbers to function spaces.
concepts.formal_gloss: The first-order axiom system (extensionality, pairing, union, power set, infinity, separation, replacement, foundation, choice) serving as the standard foundation of mathematics.

resources.title: Set Theory — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy

concepts.name: Bolzano–Weierstrass Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Trap infinitely many points in a bounded stretch of the line and they must pile up somewhere — you can always pull out a thread of them that converges.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every bounded sequence in R (or R^n) has a convergent subsequence; equivalently, closed bounded subsets of R^n are sequentially compact.

concepts.name: Diagonalization & Cantor's Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Walk down the diagonal of any claimed complete list and flip each entry — you build something the list provably missed, so no list was complete.
concepts.formal_gloss: The self-referential technique proving R uncountable and Cantor's theorem that |A| < |P(A)| for every set A, yielding an endless hierarchy of infinities.

resources.title: Numberphile — Infinity is bigger than you think

sources: Gowers' emphasis on diagonalization as a master technique

concepts.name: Cauchy Sequence
concepts.intuition_gloss: A sequence whose terms huddle ever closer to each other — you can tell it's settling down without knowing where it's headed.
concepts.formal_gloss: A sequence (a_n) such that for every ε > 0 there exists N with |a_m - a_n| < ε for all m, n > N; in R, Cauchy is equivalent to convergent.
sources: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis Ch. 3

concepts.name: Central Limit Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Add up enough independent random nudges — whatever their individual shape — and the total's distribution morphs into the bell curve.
concepts.formal_gloss: For i.i.d. X_i with mean μ and finite variance σ², (X̄_n - μ)√n/σ converges in distribution to the standard normal as n → ∞.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — But what is the Central Limit Theorem?

concepts.name: Circle
concepts.intuition_gloss: All the points at one fixed distance from a center — the roundest shape, with the number pi hiding in its measurements.
concepts.formal_gloss: The locus {P : d(P, O) = r} for a center O and radius r — the boundary curve, of circumference 2πr, of the disk (the filled region) whose area is πr².

concepts.name: Curvature
concepts.intuition_gloss: A number saying how sharply something bends at each point — zero for flat, positive for sphere-like domes, negative for saddle shapes.
concepts.formal_gloss: For curves, the rate of turning of the tangent per unit arc length; for surfaces, Gaussian curvature, which Gauss's Theorema Egregium shows is intrinsic — detectable by measurements within the surface alone.

resources.title: But why is a sphere's surface area four times its shadow? (3Blue1Brown)

sources: Gauss, Disquisitiones generales circa superficies curvas (1827)
Gauss-Bonnet theorem as bridge to topology
leverage: the pivotal concept linking local calculus to global shape, and geometry to general relativity

concepts.name: Floating-Point Arithmetic
concepts.intuition_gloss: Computers store only finitely many digits, so every real number gets snapped to the nearest representable one — and the tiny snapping errors can pile up.
concepts.formal_gloss: The IEEE 754 representation of reals as sign, exponent, and significand of fixed width, with each arithmetic operation correctly rounded to relative precision machine-epsilon.

resources.title: Goldberg — What Every Computer Scientist Should Know About Floating-Point Arithmetic

sources: Trefethen & Bau, Numerical Linear Algebra lecture 13
Higham, Accuracy and Stability of Numerical Algorithms

concepts.name: Holomorphic Function
concepts.intuition_gloss: Approach a point from any direction and the derivative comes out the same single complex number — locally the map is just one rotation-and-scaling — a constraint so rigid it forces the function to be infinitely smooth and locally a power series.
concepts.formal_gloss: A function f: C → C complex-differentiable on an open set, equivalently satisfying the Cauchy–Riemann equations; holomorphic implies analytic.

concepts.name: Least Squares
concepts.intuition_gloss: When no exact solution exists, take the shadow: find the answer that misses all the equations by the smallest total amount.
concepts.formal_gloss: The minimizer of ||Ax − b||² over x, characterized by the normal equations AᵀAx = Aᵀb, i.e. projecting b orthogonally onto the column space of A.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 16: Projection matrices and least squares

sources: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra ch. 4

concepts.name: Linear ODEs & Superposition
concepts.intuition_gloss: For linear rules of change, solutions add: find a few exponential building blocks and every solution is a mix of them.
concepts.formal_gloss: An ODE of the form y^(n) + a_{n-1}(t)y^(n-1) + ... + a_0(t)y = g(t); homogeneous solutions form a vector space, and constant-coefficient cases are solved by the roots of the characteristic polynomial via y = e^{rt}.

concepts.name: Orthogonal & Unitary Matrices
concepts.intuition_gloss: Transformations that move space rigidly — rotations and reflections that never stretch, shrink, or shear anything.
concepts.formal_gloss: A real matrix Q with QᵀQ = I (complex: U*U = I), i.e. one preserving inner products; such matrices form the groups O(n) and U(n).

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 17: Orthogonal matrices and Gram-Schmidt

concepts.name: Projective Geometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: The geometry of perspective drawing, where parallel train tracks really do meet — at horizon points added to the plane.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of properties invariant under projection, on the projective space obtained by adjoining points at infinity; formally, the space of lines through the origin of a vector space.
sources: Princeton Companion (projective geometry as unifying frame)
leverage: unifies the conics, underlies algebraic geometry and computer vision

concepts.name: Quadratic Equation
concepts.intuition_gloss: The first equation with a curve in it — a parabola — whose solutions are captured once and for all by a single closed formula.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0 with a nonzero, solved by completing the square to give x = (-b ± sqrt(b^2 - 4ac)) / 2a, with the discriminant governing the nature of the roots.

concepts.name: Rank
concepts.intuition_gloss: The number of genuinely independent directions a matrix's output can fill — how much of the target space it actually reaches.
concepts.formal_gloss: The dimension of the column space (equivalently row space) of a matrix, equal to the number of pivots in its echelon form.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06 video lectures (see Lectures 7-8: solving Ax = 0 and Ax = b, rank)

sources: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra ch. 3

concepts.name: Rubber-Sheet Geometry (What Topology Studies)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Imagine every shape is made of infinitely stretchy rubber: squash it, stretch it, bend it — but never cut or glue — and ask what stays the same. A coffee mug and a donut are the 'same' this way; a ball and a donut are not.
concepts.formal_gloss: The informal entry point to topology: studying properties of shapes that survive continuous deformation (stretching and bending without cutting or gluing), later made precise by continuity, homeomorphism, and topological invariants.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — This open problem taught me what topology is

sources: standard popular framing ('rubber-sheet geometry') traced to Möbius/Listing-era descriptions
first-lecture framing in Armstrong 'Basic Topology' and popular treatments

concepts.name: Stability & Lyapunov Functions
concepts.intuition_gloss: Nudge a marble in a bowl and it settles back; nudge it on a dome and it flees — stability asks which kind of resting point you have.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equilibrium is Lyapunov stable if nearby trajectories stay nearby and asymptotically stable if they also converge; a function decreasing along trajectories (a Lyapunov function) certifies stability without solving the equations.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Systems of ODEs & the Matrix Exponential
concepts.intuition_gloss: When several changing quantities push on each other, bundle them into one vector and let a matrix run the whole dance at once.
concepts.formal_gloss: A vector ODE x' = f(x) on R^n; the linear case x' = Ax is solved by x(t) = e^{At}x_0, with growth, decay, rotation, and shear classified by the eigenvalues of A.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — How (and why) to raise e to the power of a matrix

concepts.name: Uniform Convergence
concepts.intuition_gloss: A sequence of functions closing in on a target curve everywhere at the same speed, so good properties like continuity survive the limit.
concepts.formal_gloss: f_n → f uniformly if sup_x |f_n(x) - f(x)| → 0; uniform limits preserve continuity and allow interchange of limit with integral.

concepts.name: Area & Volume
concepts.intuition_gloss: How much flat space a shape covers, or how much room a solid fills, measured by tiling it with unit squares or cubes.
concepts.formal_gloss: A finitely additive, congruence-invariant measure on plane or solid regions, normalized so the unit square (or cube) has measure 1.

resources.title: But why is a sphere's surface area four times its shadow? (3Blue1Brown)

sources: Euclid, Elements Books I-II, XII (method of exhaustion)
Hilbert's third problem (limits of dissection) as depth marker

concepts.name: Bernoulli Trials & Binomial Distribution
concepts.intuition_gloss: Repeated independent yes/no experiments, and the resulting counts of successes — the fruit-fly of probability.
concepts.formal_gloss: For n independent trials with success probability p, the number of successes X satisfies P(X = k) = C(n,k) p^k (1-p)^(n-k).

concepts.name: Compactness
concepts.intuition_gloss: A space that is 'small' in a deep sense — you can never sneak off to infinity or to a missing edge, and any infinite scattering of points must pile up somewhere.
concepts.formal_gloss: A space is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover; in R^n this coincides with being closed and bounded (Heine-Borel), and in metric spaces it is equivalent to sequential compactness — every sequence has a convergent subsequence (Bolzano-Weierstrass). Continuous images of compact spaces are compact.

concepts.name: Covariance & Correlation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A single number capturing whether two quantities tend to move together, apart, or ignore each other.
concepts.formal_gloss: Cov(X,Y) = E[(X-E[X])(Y-E[Y])]; correlation ρ = Cov(X,Y)/(σ_X σ_Y) ∈ [-1,1] measures the strength of linear association.

concepts.name: Entropy & Information
concepts.intuition_gloss: A measure of surprise: how many yes/no questions, on average, it takes to pin down the outcome of a random source.
concepts.formal_gloss: H(X) = -Σ p(x) log p(x), the expected log-loss of a distribution; relative entropy (KL divergence) D(p‖q) measures the inefficiency of assuming q when the truth is p.
sources: Shannon, A Mathematical Theory of Communication (1948)
Cover & Thomas, Elements of Information Theory ch. 2

concepts.name: Euclid's Infinitude of Primes
concepts.intuition_gloss: Multiply any finite list of primes together and add 1 — the result dodges every prime on the list, so no list of primes can ever be complete.
concepts.formal_gloss: There are infinitely many primes: given any finite set of primes p1,...,pk, every prime divisor of p1*p2*...*pk + 1 lies outside the set (Euclid, Elements IX.20).

resources.title: Silverman, A Friendly Introduction to Number Theory (free chapters)

sources: the level-2 on-ramp to prime-distribution promised by its sources note

concepts.name: Extreme Value Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: A continuous function on a closed, bounded stretch must actually reach a highest and a lowest point — no peak forever just out of reach.
concepts.formal_gloss: A continuous real-valued function on a compact set attains its maximum and minimum; on R, compact means closed and bounded (Heine–Borel).

concepts.name: Field Extension
concepts.intuition_gloss: Growing a number system just enough to house a root it was missing — like adjoining sqrt(2) to the rationals — and measuring how much bigger the world got.
concepts.formal_gloss: A field K containing a field F, viewed as an F-vector space whose dimension [K:F] is the degree; elements algebraic over F have minimal polynomials, and splitting fields adjoin all roots of a given polynomial.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

concepts.name: Geodesic
concepts.intuition_gloss: The straightest possible path in a curved world — what a stretched string or a long-haul flight route follows.
concepts.formal_gloss: A locally length-minimizing curve, equivalently one whose tangent vector is parallel-transported along itself, satisfying the geodesic differential equation.
sources: do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

concepts.name: Dimension
concepts.intuition_gloss: How many independent directions a space offers — a line has one, a tabletop two, our world three, and mathematics keeps climbing without stopping.
concepts.formal_gloss: The minimum number of coordinates needed to specify a point of a space; sharper notions — basis cardinality for vector spaces, Hausdorff dimension for fractals — refine this count and are developed under their own concepts.

resources.title: Flatland: A Romance of Many Dimensions (Abbott, Project Gutenberg)

concepts.name: Graph Coloring
concepts.intuition_gloss: Paint the dots of a network so no wire joins two dots of the same color, using as few colors as possible — maps only ever need four.
concepts.formal_gloss: A proper k-coloring assigns k colors to vertices so adjacent vertices differ; the chromatic number χ(G) is the minimum such k, and the Four Color Theorem states χ ≤ 4 for planar graphs.
sources: Four Color Theorem (first major computer-assisted proof, 1976)

concepts.name: Group Action
concepts.intuition_gloss: A group put to work moving the points of some object around; watching the orbits it carves out is how symmetry becomes a counting and classifying tool.
concepts.formal_gloss: A homomorphism from a group G to the permutations of a set X, written g·x; orbits partition X and the orbit-stabilizer theorem gives |orbit(x)| · |stab(x)| = |G|.

concepts.name: Ideal & Quotient Ring
concepts.intuition_gloss: The ring-world analogue of 'multiples of n': a subset that swallows multiplication, so you can mod out by it and do arithmetic with the remainders.
concepts.formal_gloss: An additive subgroup I of a ring R with rI ⊆ I for all r in R; the cosets form the quotient ring R/I, with I prime iff R/I is a domain and maximal iff R/I is a field.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

concepts.name: Isometry (Rigid Motion)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Every way to move a shape without distorting it — slides, turns, and flips — and nothing else.
concepts.formal_gloss: A distance-preserving bijection of a metric space; the isometries of the Euclidean plane are exactly translations, rotations, reflections, and glide reflections, and they form a group. Congruence, defined informally earlier, is made precise here: two figures are congruent exactly when some isometry maps one onto the other.
sources: classification of plane isometries (standard undergraduate result)
leverage: makes congruence precise and connects geometry to group theory

concepts.name: Kernel & Image
concepts.intuition_gloss: What a transformation crushes to zero, and everything it can actually produce — the two subspaces that tell its whole story.
concepts.formal_gloss: For linear T: V → W, ker T = {v : Tv = 0} is a subspace of V and im T = {Tv : v ∈ V} is a subspace of W.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Inverse matrices, column space and null space
Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 3

concepts.name: Linear Programming
concepts.intuition_gloss: Optimize a linear goal — profit, cost, throughput — over a region fenced in by linear constraints; if there's a best point, one always sits at a corner of the fence.
concepts.formal_gloss: Minimizing a linear objective c·x subject to linear constraints Ax ≤ b, x ≥ 0, over a polyhedral feasible set; solvable by the simplex method or polynomial-time interior-point methods, with a symmetric dual problem attaining the same optimum.
sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics §III.84 (The Simplex Algorithm)

concepts.name: Module
aka: structure theorem for finitely generated modules
concepts.intuition_gloss: A vector space whose scalars come from a mere ring instead of a field — a rougher habitat where scaling can crush elements to zero, as in clock arithmetic, unifying abelian groups and linear operators in one frame.
concepts.formal_gloss: An abelian group M with a ring action R × M → M satisfying the vector-space axioms over R; over a PID, finitely generated modules decompose into cyclic pieces, yielding both the classification of finite abelian groups and Jordan normal form.

concepts.name: Multiple Representations
concepts.intuition_gloss: The same idea can be a picture, a formula, a table, or a story — power comes from hopping to whichever view makes the problem easy.
concepts.formal_gloss: The skill of encoding one mathematical object in several equivalent registers (symbolic, graphical, numerical, verbal) and translating between them to expose different affordances.
sources: mathematics-education research (Lesh, Duval on registers of representation)

concepts.name: Multiplicative Order & Primitive Roots
concepts.intuition_gloss: Keep multiplying a number by itself on the mod-n clock and watch how long before it first returns to 1 — some special numbers take the longest possible tour.
concepts.formal_gloss: The order of a modulo n is the least k >= 1 with a^k ≡ 1 (mod n); a primitive root is an element of order φ(n), i.e., a generator of the cyclic group (Z/nZ)*.
sources: underlies Diffie-Hellman and the discrete-log problem

concepts.name: Conditioning & Numerical Stability
concepts.intuition_gloss: Some problems amplify tiny input wobbles into huge answer swings, and some algorithms add wobbles of their own — telling these apart is the heart of trustworthy computation.
concepts.formal_gloss: Conditioning measures a problem's intrinsic sensitivity (the condition number bounds relative output change per relative input change); an algorithm is backward stable if its computed answer is exact for a nearby input.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.335J Introduction to Numerical Methods

sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics §IV.21 (Numerical Analysis, Trefethen)

concepts.name: Pigeonhole Principle
concepts.intuition_gloss: If you stuff more pigeons than holes, some hole must hold at least two pigeons — an obvious fact with astonishing consequences.
concepts.formal_gloss: If n objects are placed into m boxes with n > m, then some box contains at least ceil(n/m) objects; formally, no injection exists from a larger finite set into a smaller one.

resources.title: MIT OCW 6.042J Mathematics for Computer Science

sources: gateway existence argument in every combinatorics curriculum

concepts.name: Point Estimation & Estimators
concepts.intuition_gloss: Guessing a hidden number from noisy data with a formula — and judging the formula by how it behaves over many imagined repetitions.
concepts.formal_gloss: An estimator is a statistic θ̂(X₁..Xₙ) targeting a parameter θ, evaluated by bias E[θ̂] - θ, variance, mean squared error, and consistency as n → ∞.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

concepts.name: Polygons & Polyhedra
concepts.intuition_gloss: Shapes fenced by straight sides in the plane, and solids walled by flat faces in space — triangles to cubes to soccer balls.
concepts.formal_gloss: A polygon is a closed plane figure bounded by finitely many segments; a polyhedron is a solid bounded by finitely many polygonal faces; the regular convex cases are fully classified (n-gons; the five Platonic solids).

concepts.name: Factoring & Roots of Polynomials
concepts.intuition_gloss: Breaking a polynomial into simpler multiplied pieces, where each piece points to a place the graph crosses zero.
concepts.formal_gloss: Expressing a polynomial as a product of lower-degree polynomials; by the factor theorem, r is a root of p iff (x - r) divides p, so a degree-n polynomial has at most n roots.

concepts.name: Primality Testing
concepts.intuition_gloss: Deciding whether a huge number is prime without trying to factor it — fast randomized tests make 600-digit verdicts routine.
concepts.formal_gloss: Algorithms deciding primality: Fermat/Miller-Rabin probabilistic tests based on a^(n-1) mod n, and the deterministic polynomial-time AKS algorithm (2002) placing PRIMES in P.
sources: Crandall & Pomerance, Prime Numbers: A Computational Perspective

concepts.name: Reading & Writing Proofs
concepts.intuition_gloss: A proof is a piece of writing aimed at a human reader — learning to read one slowly and write one clearly is a craft of its own.
concepts.formal_gloss: The conventions for communicating proofs — statement-proof structure, quantifier discipline, appropriate granularity of steps — and the skill of verifying and reconstructing others' arguments.

resources.title: Mathematical Writing (Knuth, Larrabee, Roberts) — course notes
On writing — Terence Tao's advice pages

concepts.name: Recurrence Relations
concepts.intuition_gloss: Define each term of a sequence from the ones before it — like Fibonacci's rabbits — then hunt for a closed-form shortcut.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equation expressing a_n in terms of earlier terms; linear recurrences with constant coefficients are solved via characteristic roots or generating functions.

resources.title: Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, 2nd ed., ch. 1-2 (Addison-Wesley)

sources: Concrete Mathematics ch. 1 (Tower of Hanoi opener)
discrete mirror of differential equations — high leverage into CS

concepts.name: Recursive Definition
concepts.intuition_gloss: Define a thing by how it's built from smaller versions of itself — factorial, Fibonacci, and every syntax tree are made this way.
concepts.formal_gloss: Specifying a function or set by base cases and rules generating new values from prior ones, justified by the recursion theorem on well-founded structures.
sources: close pairing with induction in Book of Proof

concepts.name: Sieve of Eratosthenes
concepts.intuition_gloss: Cross out every multiple of each prime in turn, and the numbers left standing are the primes.
concepts.formal_gloss: An algorithm listing all primes up to N by iteratively marking multiples of each prime p <= sqrt(N), in O(N log log N) operations; the prototype for analytic sieve methods.
sources: gateway to sieve theory (Princeton Companion IV.2 Analytic Number Theory)

concepts.name: Similarity
concepts.intuition_gloss: Same shape, different size — one figure is a photographic enlargement of the other.
concepts.formal_gloss: Two figures are similar iff one is the image of the other under a similarity transformation (an isometry composed with a uniform dilation), giving equal angles and proportional lengths; the ratios involved are generically irrational, so the theory lives in the real numbers.
sources: incommensurability of the square's diagonal (why ratios need R, not Q)

concepts.name: Spectral Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Every symmetric transformation is secretly just stretches along some set of perpendicular axes — no rotation or shear hiding anywhere.
concepts.formal_gloss: A real symmetric (complex self-adjoint) operator has an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors with real eigenvalues: A = QΛQᵀ with Q orthogonal, Λ diagonal.

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 7
MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 25: Symmetric matrices and positive definiteness

concepts.name: Surfaces (Torus, Möbius Band, Klein Bottle)
concepts.intuition_gloss: The two-dimensional zoo of topology — spheres, donuts with any number of holes, one-sided bands and bottles — each a world that looks flat up close.
concepts.formal_gloss: A surface is a 2-dimensional topological manifold; classic examples are the sphere, the n-holed torus, the Möbius band, the Klein bottle, and the real projective plane.
sources: standard intuitive entry point in popular and undergraduate treatments

concepts.name: Well-Ordering
concepts.intuition_gloss: An ordering with no bottomless descents: every nonempty collection has a definite least member, which is exactly what makes induction-style arguments land.
concepts.formal_gloss: A total order in which every nonempty subset has a least element; N is well-ordered, and this property underwrites induction and minimal-counterexample proofs. (Distinct from the well-ordering theorem — the AC-equivalent that every set can be well-ordered — listed under axiom-of-choice.)

concepts.name: Affine Geometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: The geometry of shadows cast by a distant sun: parallel lines stay parallel and midpoints stay midpoints, but lengths and angles are free to change.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of properties invariant under affine transformations x ↦ Ax + b with A invertible — collinearity, parallelism, and ratios of lengths along parallel lines are preserved, while distances and angles are not; the middle rung between Euclidean and projective geometry.
sources: Klein, Erlangen program (1872) — the middle term of Euclidean ⊂ affine ⊂ projective

concepts.name: Arithmetic Functions & Möbius Inversion
concepts.intuition_gloss: Functions defined on the whole numbers — like 'count the divisors' or 'sum them' — that inherit the primes' multiplication structure and can be un-summed by a clever inversion.
concepts.formal_gloss: Functions f: N → C, with the multiplicative ones determined by prime-power values; under Dirichlet convolution they form a ring in which Möbius inversion recovers f from g(n) = Σ_{d|n} f(d).

resources.title: Apostol, Introduction to Analytic Number Theory, ch. 2

sources: the algebraic front end of analytic number theory

concepts.name: Axiom of Choice
concepts.intuition_gloss: The claim that from infinitely many nonempty bins you can always pick one item from each, even with no rule for choosing — harmless-sounding, wildly powerful.
concepts.formal_gloss: For every family of nonempty sets there exists a choice function selecting one element from each; equivalent to Zorn's lemma and the well-ordering theorem, and independent of ZF.

resources.title: The Axiom of Choice — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy

sources: Princeton Companion III.1 (The Axiom of Choice)

concepts.name: Normed & Banach Spaces
concepts.intuition_gloss: Treat entire functions as single points in an infinite-dimensional space with a notion of length, where analysis and linear algebra fuse.
concepts.formal_gloss: A vector space with a norm inducing a complete metric; the setting of functional analysis, where linear operators between such spaces are studied.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.102 Introduction to Functional Analysis

concepts.name: Bayes' Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: The rule for updating a belief when evidence arrives — flip 'probability of evidence given cause' into 'probability of cause given evidence'.
concepts.formal_gloss: P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A)/P(B), decomposing a posterior probability into likelihood times prior over marginal evidence.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

concepts.name: Bijective & Double-Counting Proof
concepts.intuition_gloss: Prove two counts are equal by pairing the objects off one-to-one, or by counting the same pile from two different directions.
concepts.formal_gloss: A proof that |A| = |B| by exhibiting an explicit bijection A → B, or that two expressions agree by showing both enumerate the same set.

resources.title: Benjamin & Quinn, Proofs that Really Count (MAA)

sources: Benjamin & Quinn (MAA, devoted entirely to the technique)
the signature proof style of enumerative combinatorics

concepts.name: Brownian Motion
concepts.intuition_gloss: The jittery path of a pollen grain kicked by invisible molecules — pure continuous randomness, the limiting shape of ever-finer random walks.
concepts.formal_gloss: The continuous-time process with B₀ = 0, independent stationary Gaussian increments B_t - B_s ~ N(0, t-s), and continuous but nowhere-differentiable sample paths.

resources.title: Random (Virtual Laboratories in Probability) — Brownian motion

concepts.name: Ordered Pair & Cartesian Product
concepts.intuition_gloss: Pair up every item from one bag with every item from another, like all (row, column) squares on a chessboard.
concepts.formal_gloss: A × B is the set of ordered pairs (a, b) with a in A and b in B; the ordered pair is codable in pure set theory as {{a},{a,b}} (Kuratowski).

concepts.name: Cauchy's Integral Theorem & Residues
concepts.intuition_gloss: Integrating a well-behaved complex function around a closed loop gives zero — unless the loop encircles singularities, whose 'residues' alone determine the answer.
