concepts.formal_gloss: ∮ f dz = 0 for f holomorphic on a simply connected domain; more generally the integral equals 2πi times the sum of residues at enclosed poles, weighted by winding numbers.
sources: Powers analytic number theory — high leverage

concepts.name: Chaos & Sensitive Dependence
concepts.intuition_gloss: A perfectly deterministic system whose future is still unforecastable, because today's tiniest rounding error snowballs exponentially.
concepts.formal_gloss: Aperiodic long-term behavior in a deterministic system exhibiting sensitive dependence on initial conditions, typically together with dense periodic orbits and topological mixing.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Chinese Remainder Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: If clocks of coprime sizes each show a chosen time, exactly one number on the combined mega-clock matches them all.
concepts.formal_gloss: For pairwise coprime moduli n1..nk, the ring Z/(n1...nk)Z is isomorphic to the product of the rings Z/niZ, so any system x ≡ ai (mod ni) has a unique solution mod the product.
sources: undergrad NT and abstract algebra syllabi (ring-product form)

concepts.name: Computational Complexity
concepts.intuition_gloss: Measuring how the cost of a recipe explodes as the problem gets bigger — some recipes scale gracefully, others become hopeless fast.
concepts.formal_gloss: The classification of problems by the asymptotic resources (time, space) sufficient to solve them, expressed in O-notation and complexity classes such as P, NP, and PSPACE.

resources.title: Wikipedia — about Sipser's Introduction to the Theory of Computation (the standard text)

sources: Sipser, Introduction to the Theory of Computation

concepts.name: Conditional Expectation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Your best guess of one random quantity after seeing another — an average taken inside each slice of what you know, which is itself random because the slice you land in is.
concepts.formal_gloss: E[X|Y] is the random variable g(Y) with g(y) = E[X | Y = y]; measure-theoretically, E[X|F] is the F-measurable random variable satisfying ∫_A E[X|F] dP = ∫_A X dP for all A ∈ F — equivalently, the orthogonal projection of X onto L²(F). The tower property E[E[X|F]] = E[X] makes it the workhorse of modern probability.
sources: Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples ch. 4

concepts.name: Conjecture
concepts.intuition_gloss: A pattern you've spotted and believe is always true, stated precisely and put on trial — mathematics grows by making and testing such guesses.
concepts.formal_gloss: A precisely stated proposition believed true on the basis of evidence or heuristics but not yet proved; it becomes a theorem when proved or is refuted by counterexample.

resources.title: Mathematics and Plausible Reasoning (Polya) — Wikipedia overview

concepts.name: Continued Fractions
concepts.intuition_gloss: Write a number as a nested staircase of fractions, and each landing is the best possible fraction approximation so far.
concepts.formal_gloss: Expressions a0 + 1/(a1 + 1/(a2 + ...)); the convergents p_n/q_n give best rational approximations, and the expansion is eventually periodic iff the number is a quadratic irrational.

concepts.name: Convex Function
concepts.intuition_gloss: A bowl-shaped function — every chord lies above the graph, so any local bottom is the global bottom.
concepts.formal_gloss: f is convex if f(tx + (1-t)y) ≤ tf(x) + (1-t)f(y) for t in [0,1]; for twice-differentiable f, equivalent to f'' ≥ 0.
sources: Central to optimization and inequalities (Jensen) — high cross-territory leverage

concepts.name: Convexity
concepts.intuition_gloss: A shape with no dents: the straight path between any two of its points never leaves it.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set C in a real vector space is convex if tx + (1−t)y ∈ C for all x, y ∈ C and t ∈ [0,1], supporting separation, Helly, and extreme-point theorems.
sources: leverage: the dividing line between tractable and intractable optimization

concepts.name: Covering Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: A space that lies over another like an unrolled spiral over a circle — many identical sheets projecting down, unwinding the loops below.
concepts.formal_gloss: A map p : X̃ → X where every point of X has a neighborhood whose preimage is a disjoint union of homeomorphic copies; covering spaces of a nice space correspond to subgroups of its fundamental group (Galois correspondence).

concepts.name: Discretization
concepts.intuition_gloss: Chop the smooth, continuous world into a finite grid of points a computer can handle, accepting a controlled error for the ability to compute at all.
concepts.formal_gloss: The replacement of a continuous problem by a finite-dimensional one — derivatives by difference quotients, domains by meshes, functions by sample vectors — with error that vanishes as the mesh is refined (consistency plus stability yielding convergence).

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.085 Computational Science and Engineering I (Strang)

concepts.name: Dual Space & Linear Functionals
concepts.intuition_gloss: The space of all measuring devices — every consistent way of reading off a single number from vectors is itself a vector.
concepts.formal_gloss: The space V* of linear maps V → F; finite-dimensional V is isomorphic to V*, and an inner product identifies them canonically (Riesz representation).

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 3F

concepts.name: Elliptic Curves
concepts.intuition_gloss: A cubic curve where a chord-and-tangent game turns the points into an addition system — the meeting point of geometry, arithmetic, and cryptography.
concepts.formal_gloss: A smooth projective curve y^2 = x^3 + ax + b (char ≠ 2,3) with a marked point, whose points over any field form an abelian group; over Q the group is finitely generated (Mordell-Weil).

resources.title: Silverman, The Arithmetic of Elliptic Curves

sources: Princeton Companion III.21 & IV Arithmetic Geometry
BSD is a Clay Millennium Problem

concepts.name: Euler Characteristic
concepts.intuition_gloss: Count vertices minus edges plus faces of any honest drawing on a shape and you always get the same number — 2 for a sphere, 0 for a donut — no matter how you draw it.
concepts.formal_gloss: For any polyhedron, or any graph drawn on a surface so that the faces are disks, χ = V - E + F; the answer depends only on the underlying surface (2 for the sphere, 2 - 2g for the g-holed torus), not on the particular drawing — the prototype of a topological invariant, later recomputed in full generality by homology.
sources: Euler's polyhedron formula as classical entry point (Lakatos 'Proofs and Refutations')

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
concepts.intuition_gloss: Accumulating a quantity and measuring its rate of change are inverse operations — area under a rate curve is total change.
concepts.formal_gloss: If f is continuous on [a,b], then F(x) = ∫_a^x f(t)dt satisfies F' = f, and ∫_a^b f = G(b) - G(a) for any antiderivative G of f.

concepts.name: Gaussian Integers
concepts.intuition_gloss: Whole numbers spread out on a grid in the complex plane, where 5 stops being prime because it splits as (2+i)(2−i).
concepts.formal_gloss: The ring Z[i] = {a + bi : a,b in Z}, a Euclidean domain with unique factorization; an odd prime p splits in Z[i] iff p ≡ 1 (mod 4), yielding Fermat's two-squares theorem.

resources.title: Ireland & Rosen, ch. 1 (unique factorization in Z[i])

sources: the canonical first example of extending arithmetic beyond Z

concepts.name: Gradient Descent
concepts.intuition_gloss: Standing on a foggy hillside, repeatedly step in the direction of steepest downhill — the simple idea that trains nearly every modern AI model.
concepts.formal_gloss: The iteration x_{k+1} = x_k - η∇f(x_k) minimizing a differentiable f, with step size η controlling the trade-off between progress and overshoot; stochastic variants replace the gradient by an unbiased sample estimate.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Gradient descent, how neural networks learn

sources: deep learning curricula (Goodfellow et al. ch. 4, 8)

concepts.name: Gram–Schmidt Process
concepts.intuition_gloss: Straighten a skewed set of arrows one at a time — subtract each one's shadow on the previous ones, then rescale to length one.
concepts.formal_gloss: An algorithm converting a linearly independent list into an orthonormal list with the same span, by successive orthogonal projection and normalization; it is the algorithm underlying the QR factorization (see matrix-factorization).

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 17: Orthogonal matrices and Gram-Schmidt

concepts.name: Graph Algorithms
concepts.intuition_gloss: The standard toolkit for questions about networks — fastest route, cheapest wiring, maximum throughput — each answered by systematically exploring dots and lines.
concepts.formal_gloss: Algorithms on graphs including traversal (BFS/DFS), single-source shortest paths (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford), minimum spanning trees (Kruskal, Prim), and maximum-flow computation (Ford-Fulkerson) — the algorithmic counterpart of the flow and matching theory (max-flow min-cut duality) owned by the discrete-combinatorics territory.

concepts.name: Harmonic Oscillator
concepts.intuition_gloss: A weight bobbing on a spring — the universal model of anything that wobbles around a resting point, from bridges to molecules to circuits.
concepts.formal_gloss: The linear second-order ODE x'' + 2ζω x' + ω²x = F(t), with sinusoidal solutions, exponentially damped envelopes, and resonance under periodic forcing near ω.

concepts.name: Information Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: Once you can measure a source's surprise, hard limits follow — how far its messages can be squeezed, and how fast they can cross a noisy line and still arrive intact.
concepts.formal_gloss: Shannon's framework for communication built on entropy (the quantity itself lives in probability-statistics): the source coding theorem shows a source cannot be losslessly compressed below its entropy rate, and the noisy-channel coding theorem gives each channel a capacity below which arbitrarily reliable transmission is achievable.

resources.title: Shannon — A Mathematical Theory of Communication (1948)
Wikipedia — about Cover & Thomas, Elements of Information Theory (the standard text)

sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics §VII.6 (Reliable Transmission of Information)

concepts.name: Integer Factorization Problem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Multiplying two big primes is instant; recovering them from the product seems to need astronomical time — an asymmetry the digital world leans on.
concepts.formal_gloss: The problem of computing the prime factorization of n; best known classical algorithms (general number field sieve) are subexponential, while Shor's quantum algorithm factors in polynomial time.

concepts.name: Interpolation & Approximation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Given a handful of sample points, thread a smooth curve exactly through them — or find the simple curve that hugs a complicated function as closely as possible.
concepts.formal_gloss: Constructing a function from a simple class (polynomials, piecewise polynomials, trigonometric sums) that matches given data exactly (interpolation) or minimizes a norm of the error (approximation), e.g. the unique degree-n polynomial through n+1 points.

resources.title: Trefethen — Approximation Theory and Approximation Practice
Chebfun (computing with functions via Chebyshev interpolation)

sources: Trefethen, Approximation Theory and Approximation Practice
standard numerical analysis curricula (Burden & Faires ch. 3)

concepts.name: Isomorphism
concepts.intuition_gloss: A perfect relabeling showing two structures are the same thing wearing different costumes.
concepts.formal_gloss: A structure-preserving bijection between two objects whose inverse also preserves structure.

concepts.name: Measure & Lebesgue Measure
concepts.intuition_gloss: A rigorous notion of length, area, and volume robust enough to size up wildly irregular sets — with the surprise that some sets cannot be measured at all.
concepts.formal_gloss: A countably additive nonnegative function on a σ-algebra of subsets; Lebesgue measure extends length from intervals to all Borel (and Lebesgue-measurable) subsets of R.

resources.title: Terence Tao, An Introduction to Measure Theory

concepts.name: Linearization & Hyperbolicity
concepts.intuition_gloss: Zoom in on a resting point until the curved dynamics look straight, then read the local fate from a matrix's eigenvalues.
concepts.formal_gloss: Approximating x' = f(x) near an equilibrium x* by x' = Df(x*)(x − x*); the Hartman–Grobman theorem says the true phase portrait is locally conjugate to this linear one whenever no eigenvalue has zero real part.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Mean Value Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: On any smooth trip, there is an instant when your speedometer reads exactly your average speed for the whole trip.
concepts.formal_gloss: If f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), there exists c with f'(c) = (f(b) - f(a))/(b - a); the bridge from local derivative facts to global behavior. Rolle's theorem is the special case f(a) = f(b).
sources: Standard calculus and real analysis curricula

concepts.name: Models & Interpretations
concepts.intuition_gloss: A model is a concrete universe where a set of axioms comes true — and the same axioms often describe wildly different universes, which is how independence gets proven.
concepts.formal_gloss: A structure assigning meaning to a formal language so that a theory's sentences hold; Gödel's completeness theorem equates semantic consequence with formal provability in first-order logic, and the compactness and Löwenheim–Skolem theorems — its other two pillars — force theories to have models of unexpected sizes.

resources.title: Model Theory — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy

concepts.name: Monte Carlo Methods
concepts.intuition_gloss: When a problem is too tangled to compute directly, throw random darts at it and let the law of averages reveal the answer.
concepts.formal_gloss: Algorithms estimating deterministic quantities as expectations of random variables, with error decreasing as 1/sqrt(N) in the sample count by the law of large numbers and central limit theorem.

resources.title: MIT OCW 6.0002 Introduction to Computational Thinking and Data Science (Monte Carlo lectures)

concepts.name: Multiple Integral
concepts.intuition_gloss: Adding up a quantity spread over an area or volume by slicing the region into tiny tiles or bricks.
concepts.formal_gloss: The integral of f over a region in R^n, computable as iterated one-variable integrals when Fubini's theorem applies, with change of variables governed by the Jacobian determinant.

resources.title: Paul's Online Notes: Multiple Integrals (Calc III)

concepts.name: Ordinal Numbers & Transfinite Induction
concepts.intuition_gloss: Counting continued past infinity — after all the finite numbers comes ω, then ω+1, and the domino argument of induction still works along the entire transfinite line.
concepts.formal_gloss: The canonical representatives of well-order types (von Neumann: each ordinal is the set of all smaller ordinals), supporting definitions and proofs by transfinite induction and recursion.

resources.title: Vsauce — How To Count Past Infinity

concepts.name: Orientability
concepts.intuition_gloss: Whether a space has a consistent 'clockwise' everywhere — on a Möbius band an ant walking the loop comes back mirror-reversed, so it doesn't.
concepts.formal_gloss: A manifold is orientable if it admits a consistent choice of local orientation (an atlas whose transition maps preserve orientation); non-orientable surfaces contain an embedded Möbius band.
sources: classification of surfaces requires the orientable/non-orientable split

concepts.name: Peano Axioms
concepts.intuition_gloss: A short list of rules — zero exists, every number has a successor, and the induction domino principle — that pin down how the counting numbers work.
concepts.formal_gloss: The axiomatization of N via zero, an injective successor function whose range omits zero, and induction; first-order PA (with induction as an axiom schema) is the standard formal theory of arithmetic, though it admits nonstandard models — only the full second-order induction axiom characterizes N up to isomorphism.
sources: standard foundations curricula (Enderton, Open Logic Project)

concepts.name: Permutation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A shuffle of a finite set — every rearrangement decomposes into little loops that chase each other around.
concepts.formal_gloss: A bijection from a finite set to itself; the n! permutations of an n-set form the symmetric group S_n, and each factors uniquely into disjoint cycles.
sources: bridge object between counting and group theory

concepts.name: Distribution of Primes
concepts.intuition_gloss: Primes look random up close but obey a smooth global law — near N, roughly one number in every ln(N) is prime.
concepts.formal_gloss: π(x) ~ x/ln(x) (the prime number theorem, Hadamard & de la Vallée Poussin 1896); Dirichlet's theorem gives infinitely many primes in every arithmetic progression a mod n with gcd(a,n) = 1.
sources: Princeton Companion IV.2 Analytic Number Theory
Euclid's infinitude proof is the level-2 on-ramp; PNT sets the level

concepts.name: The Probabilistic Method
concepts.intuition_gloss: To prove a needle exists in the haystack, show a random grab finds one with nonzero probability — no need to ever point at it.
concepts.formal_gloss: Prove an object with property P exists by showing a suitable random construction has P with positive probability; refinements include linearity of expectation, alteration, and the Lovász Local Lemma.

resources.title: Alon & Spencer, The Probabilistic Method, 4th ed. (Wiley)

sources: Erdős 1947 (Ramsey lower bound — the founding application)

concepts.name: Proof by Cases
concepts.intuition_gloss: Split the world into a complete set of scenarios — even or odd, positive or negative — and win each battle separately to win the war.
concepts.formal_gloss: A proof that establishes a statement by partitioning all possibilities into finitely many exhaustive cases and proving the statement in each case.
sources: four-color theorem as canonical large-scale exemplar

concepts.name: Pythagorean Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: In any right triangle, the square built on the longest side exactly fills the two squares built on the shorter sides.
concepts.formal_gloss: In a right triangle with legs a, b and hypotenuse c, a² + b² = c²; equivalently, Euclidean distance is the square root of the sum of squared coordinate differences.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Pythagorean theorem (lesson, Right triangles & trigonometry unit)

sources: leverage: underlies Euclidean distance, norms, and inner products across the map

concepts.name: Pythagorean Triples
concepts.intuition_gloss: Whole-number right triangles like 3-4-5 — and a complete recipe that generates every one of them.
concepts.formal_gloss: Integer solutions of a^2 + b^2 = c^2; every primitive triple is (m^2 - n^2, 2mn, m^2 + n^2) for coprime m > n of opposite parity.

resources.title: Silverman, A Friendly Introduction to Number Theory, ch. 2-3 (free)

sources: the prototype of a completely solved Diophantine problem

concepts.name: Quadratic Forms & Positive Definiteness
concepts.intuition_gloss: The bowl-or-saddle shapes you get from squaring and mixing coordinates — positive definite means a true bowl with a single bottom.
concepts.formal_gloss: A function q(x) = xᵀAx for symmetric A; A is positive definite when q(x) > 0 for all x ≠ 0, equivalently all eigenvalues are positive.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 27: Positive definite matrices and minima

sources: Boyd & Vandenberghe, Convex Optimization appendix A

concepts.name: Quadratic Residues & Reciprocity
concepts.intuition_gloss: Whether p is a perfect square on q's clock and whether q is one on p's clock turn out to be secretly linked — Gauss's 'golden theorem'.
concepts.formal_gloss: For odd primes p and q, the Legendre symbols satisfy (p/q)(q/p) = (-1)^((p-1)/2 * (q-1)/2), governing solvability of x^2 ≡ a (mod p).

resources.title: Ireland & Rosen, A Classical Introduction to Modern Number Theory, ch. 5

sources: Princeton Companion — first step toward class field theory and Langlands

concepts.name: Separation of Variables
concepts.intuition_gloss: Herd all the y's to one side of the equation and all the t's to the other, then integrate each side separately to unlock the solution.
concepts.formal_gloss: A method solving y' = g(t)h(y) by rewriting it as dy/h(y) = g(t) dt and integrating both sides; the same divide-and-conquer idea later splits PDEs into products of one-variable problems.

concepts.name: Set Operations
concepts.intuition_gloss: Combining bags of things — merge them, keep only the overlap, or take everything outside one — pictured perfectly by Venn diagrams.
concepts.formal_gloss: The operations of union, intersection, difference, and complement on sets, obeying laws (distributivity, De Morgan) that mirror logical connectives.
sources: elementary/discrete curricula (Khan Academy, Book of Proof ch. 1)

concepts.name: Simplicial Complex & Triangulation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Build any shape out of standardized LEGO — points, segments, triangles, tetrahedra glued face-to-face — turning floppy geometry into finite, computable data.
concepts.formal_gloss: A collection of simplices glued along faces such that every face of a simplex is in the collection and intersections are common faces; a triangulation is a homeomorphism from such a complex to a space. (CW complexes are a more general construction in which cells are attached by arbitrary continuous maps.)
sources: Hatcher Ch. 0 (CW complexes) and Ch. 2 (simplicial homology)

concepts.name: Singular Value Decomposition
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any transformation whatsoever is a rotation, then pure stretches along perpendicular axes, then another rotation — the universal anatomy of a matrix.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every m×n matrix factors as A = UΣVᵀ with U, V orthogonal and Σ diagonal with nonnegative singular values; the rank-k truncation is the best rank-k approximation in the 2- and Frobenius norms.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 29: Singular value decomposition
Steve Brunton — Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) series

sources: Brunton & Kutz, Data-Driven Science and Engineering ch. 1

concepts.name: Trying Small Cases
concepts.intuition_gloss: Before wrestling the general beast, play with the tiniest examples — n equals 1, 2, 3 — and let the pattern introduce itself.
concepts.formal_gloss: The heuristic of instantiating a general problem at small or extreme parameter values to generate data, form conjectures, and reveal structure that guides the general argument.

concepts.name: Sylow Theorems
concepts.intuition_gloss: Lagrange only says a subgroup's size must divide the group's; Sylow answers back — for the largest prime-power chunk of that size, a subgroup really does exist, all copies of it are just relabelings of each other, and you can even count how many there are.
concepts.formal_gloss: For a finite group of order p^n·m with p prime and p not dividing m: subgroups of order p^n (Sylow p-subgroups) exist, any two are conjugate, and their number divides m and is congruent to 1 mod p — proved via group actions on cosets and conjugates.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

concepts.name: Tensor Product
concepts.intuition_gloss: A machine that turns two-slot multiplication into one-slot linearity: build the freest possible space where a pair of vectors 'multiplies' to a single object, and every bilinear rule you could ever write factors through it.
concepts.formal_gloss: For modules M and N over a ring R, the module M ⊗_R N equipped with a universal bilinear map M × N → M ⊗ N through which every R-bilinear map factors uniquely; the construction from which the derived functor Tor arises.
sources: Evan Chen, An Infinitely Large Napkin (tensor product chapters)

concepts.name: Visual & Diagrammatic Reasoning
concepts.intuition_gloss: Draw the thing — a good picture can carry an entire argument, turning an algebraic identity into something you simply see.
concepts.formal_gloss: The use of diagrams and spatial structure as cognitive scaffolding for conjecture and proof, from proofs-without-words to commutative diagrams, with the obligation that pictures be backed by rigorous argument.

concepts.name: Additive Number Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: Instead of asking how numbers multiply, ask what you can build by adding: is every even number two primes? Every number four squares? Questions a child can pose that have resisted proof for centuries.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of representing integers as sums from special sets (MSC 11P): Fermat's two-square and Lagrange's four-square theorems, Waring's problem on kth powers, and the open Goldbach and twin prime conjectures.

resources.title: Nathanson, Additive Number Theory: The Classical Bases (Springer GTM 164)

sources: Hardy & Wright ch. 20-21 (sums of squares, Waring)

concepts.name: Algebraic Geometry (Varieties)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Shapes carved out by polynomial equations, studied by letting the algebra of those equations speak for the geometry.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of algebraic varieties — solution sets of systems of polynomial equations — via the commutative rings of functions on them, and their scheme-theoretic generalization.
sources: leverage: engine behind Wiles's proof of Fermat's Last Theorem and modern number theory

concepts.name: Algebraic Number Fields & Ideal Factorization
concepts.intuition_gloss: Enlarge the whole numbers with roots of polynomials; unique factorization can break, but it is rescued by factoring 'ideal' numbers instead.
concepts.formal_gloss: A number field is a finite extension K/Q with ring of integers O_K, a Dedekind domain in which every nonzero ideal factors uniquely into prime ideals; the class group measures the failure of unique element factorization.

resources.title: Milne, Algebraic Number Theory (free course notes)

sources: Princeton Companion IV.1 Algebraic Numbers (Granville/Mazur treatment)

concepts.name: Asymptotics & Big-O Notation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Comparing how fast functions grow or shrink in the long run, ignoring constants and small-fry terms.
concepts.formal_gloss: f = O(g) if |f(x)| ≤ C|g(x)| for large x; the calculus of dominant behavior of functions as an argument tends to a limit.
sources: Bridges analysis to computer science and analytic number theory

concepts.name: Attractors & Strange Attractors
concepts.intuition_gloss: The shape a system's long-run behavior settles onto — a point, a loop, or in chaotic systems an infinitely detailed butterfly-shaped filigree.
concepts.formal_gloss: A compact invariant set that attracts all orbits from a neighborhood; strange attractors carry fractal structure and chaotic dynamics, the Lorenz system being the canonical example.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Bayesian Inference
concepts.intuition_gloss: Treat the unknown itself as uncertain: start with a belief, let each datapoint reshape it via Bayes' rule, and read answers off the updated belief.
concepts.formal_gloss: Inference placing a prior distribution π(θ) on parameters and computing the posterior π(θ|x) ∝ L(θ; x)π(θ), from which estimates and credible intervals are derived.
sources: Gelman et al., Bayesian Data Analysis ch. 1-2

concepts.name: Biconditional, Set-Equality & Uniqueness Proofs
concepts.intuition_gloss: Some claims are two-way streets: prove 'iff' by walking both directions, prove two sets equal by showing each fits inside the other, and prove uniqueness by supposing two winners and forcing them to be the same.
concepts.formal_gloss: Standard proof patterns for non-conditional statements: P iff Q via the two implications P implies Q and Q implies P; A = B via the double inclusion A subset of B and B subset of A; and existence-and-uniqueness via exhibiting a witness and showing any two witnesses coincide.

resources.title: If and only if — Wikipedia (proving biconditionals)
Book of Proof (Hammack), chs. 7-8 (non-conditional statements, proofs involving sets)

sources: Velleman, How to Prove It (biconditional and uniqueness proof strategies)

concepts.name: Bifurcation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Turn a dial slowly and the system's whole repertoire can suddenly reorganize — new resting points appear from nowhere, or a steady state bursts into oscillation.
concepts.formal_gloss: A parameter value at which the topological structure of a system's phase portrait changes, classified locally into normal forms such as saddle-node, transcritical, pitchfork, and Hopf.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Binary Quadratic Forms
concepts.intuition_gloss: Which whole numbers can a two-variable recipe like x² + 5y² produce? Gauss sorted these recipes into families and found the families themselves can be multiplied — a hidden group counting how badly unique factorization fails.
concepts.formal_gloss: Forms ax^2 + bxy + cy^2 with discriminant b^2 - 4ac; SL2(Z)-equivalence classes of a fixed discriminant form a finite abelian group under Gauss composition, isomorphic to the ideal class group of the corresponding quadratic order.

resources.title: Cox, Primes of the Form x² + ny² (Wiley)

sources: Cox, Primes of the Form x²+ny²
the classical road to class numbers, MSC 11E

concepts.name: Relation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any way of connecting some things to other things — 'is taller than', 'divides', 'is a friend of' — recorded as a set of pairs.
concepts.formal_gloss: A binary relation from A to B is a subset of A × B; functions, orders, and equivalences are relations with extra properties.
sources: standard discrete/transition curricula (Book of Proof ch. 11)

concepts.name: Boolean Algebra
concepts.intuition_gloss: The two-valued arithmetic of true and false, where the same laws govern logic connectives, set operations, and the circuits inside every computer.
concepts.formal_gloss: An algebraic structure with meet, join, and complement satisfying distributive and complementation laws, of which propositional logic and power-set algebras are canonical instances.
sources: bridge role noted in Princeton Companion and CS curricula

concepts.name: Bootstrap & Resampling
concepts.intuition_gloss: When you can't resurvey the world, resample your own data with replacement thousands of times and watch how the answer wobbles.
concepts.formal_gloss: Estimating the sampling distribution of a statistic by drawing repeated samples with replacement from the empirical distribution, yielding standard errors and confidence intervals without closed-form theory.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

sources: Efron & Tibshirani, An Introduction to the Bootstrap

concepts.name: Borsuk-Ulam Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: At any moment there are two exactly opposite points on Earth with the same temperature and the same pressure — squash a sphere flat however continuously you like, and some pair of antipodes must land on the same spot.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every continuous map from the n-sphere to R^n sends some pair of antipodal points to the same point; equivalently, no continuous map S^n → S^(n-1) commutes with the antipodal map. It implies Brouwer's fixed-point theorem and the ham sandwich theorem, and yields combinatorial results like the necklace-splitting theorem.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Using topology for discrete problems | The Borsuk-Ulam theorem and stolen necklaces

sources: Matoušek 'Using the Borsuk-Ulam Theorem' (canonical treatment of combinatorial applications)

concepts.name: Brouwer Fixed-Point Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Stir a cup of coffee however you like: some point of the liquid ends up exactly where it started — any continuous self-map of a disk must fix a point.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every continuous map from a closed n-ball to itself has a fixed point; equivalently, there is no continuous retraction of the ball onto its boundary sphere.
sources: Hatcher Ch. 1 (application of π1) and Ch. 2 (via homology)

concepts.name: Calculus of Variations
concepts.intuition_gloss: Instead of finding the best point, find the best curve — among all paths a hanging chain or a light ray could take, nature picks the one no tiny wiggle can improve.
concepts.formal_gloss: Optimization of functionals J[y] = ∫ L(x, y, y') dx over spaces of functions; stationary paths satisfy the Euler–Lagrange equation ∂L/∂y - d/dx(∂L/∂y') = 0.

concepts.name: Cantor–Schröder–Bernstein Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: If each of two bags can be tucked whole into the other without doubling anything up, then the two bags can in fact be perfectly paired off — same size, guaranteed.
concepts.formal_gloss: If there exist injections f: A → B and g: B → A, then there exists a bijection between A and B; hence |A| ≤ |B| and |B| ≤ |A| imply |A| = |B|, provable without the axiom of choice.

concepts.name: Cardinal Numbers & the Aleph Hierarchy
concepts.intuition_gloss: Sizes of infinity made into numbers of their own: ℵ₀ for the countable, then ℵ₁, ℵ₂, and so on up an endless ladder, each rung a strictly bigger infinity you can add, multiply, and exponentiate.
concepts.formal_gloss: Cardinals are canonical representatives of equinumerosity classes (under AC, the initial ordinals), forming the aleph hierarchy ℵ₀ < ℵ₁ < ...; cardinal arithmetic makes |P(A)| = 2^|A| precise, and the value of 2^ℵ₀ is the continuum problem.

resources.title: Set Theory — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy

concepts.name: Catalan Numbers
concepts.intuition_gloss: One sequence — 1, 1, 2, 5, 14, 42 — keeps turning up wherever you count balanced parentheses, mountain-shaped lattice paths, triangulated polygons, or binary trees.
concepts.formal_gloss: C_n = C(2n,n)/(n+1) counts Dyck paths of length 2n, triangulations of an (n+2)-gon, binary trees with n internal nodes, and hundreds of other families; it satisfies the convolution recurrence C_(n+1) = sum of C_i C_(n-i) with generating function (1 - sqrt(1-4x))/(2x).

resources.title: Stanley, Enumerative Combinatorics (free online editions; Catalan interpretations in vol. 2 / the Catalan addendum)

sources: Stanley EC2 exercise 6.19 and the Catalan addendum
the most famous counted family in combinatorics

concepts.name: Category Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: Study objects purely by the arrows between them — forget what things are made of and track only how they map to each other, revealing the same patterns across all of mathematics.
concepts.formal_gloss: The theory of categories (objects and composable morphisms), functors between them, and natural transformations, providing a structural alternative language for foundations.

resources.title: Category Theory — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy

sources: contested placement: also claimed by algebra; flagged per house rules

concepts.name: Fundamental Inequalities
concepts.intuition_gloss: The workhorse bounds of mathematics: a detour is never shorter than the straight path (triangle), evening out numbers only grows their product (AM–GM), and a dot product can never beat the product of the lengths (Cauchy–Schwarz).
concepts.formal_gloss: |x + y| ≤ |x| + |y|; (a_1 ··· a_n)^{1/n} ≤ (a_1 + ... + a_n)/n for nonnegative a_i; and |⟨u, v⟩| ≤ ‖u‖‖v‖, with equality iff u and v are parallel.

concepts.name: Cayley–Hamilton Theorem & Minimal Polynomial
concepts.intuition_gloss: Feed a matrix into its own characteristic polynomial and you get zero — every transformation obeys the very equation its stretch factors solve.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every square matrix A satisfies p(A) = 0 where p is its characteristic polynomial; the minimal polynomial of A is the monic polynomial of least degree annihilating A, and it divides the characteristic polynomial.

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 8

concepts.name: Classification of Surfaces
concepts.intuition_gloss: The complete map of all possible closed surfaces: every one is a sphere, a donut with some number of holes, or a sphere with some number of Möbius-style cross-caps — nothing else exists.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every compact connected surface without boundary is homeomorphic to a sphere, a connected sum of g tori, or a connected sum of k projective planes, and these are distinguished by orientability and Euler characteristic.
sources: flagship 'complete classification' example in topology curricula

concepts.name: Closure, Interior & Boundary
concepts.intuition_gloss: Every region of a space splits into its solid inside, its skin, and everything it can touch — the boundary is exactly where a set meets its outside.
concepts.formal_gloss: For a subset A of a topological space, the interior is the largest open set inside A, the closure is the smallest closed set containing A, and the boundary is the closure minus the interior.

concepts.name: Combinatorial Designs & Latin Squares
concepts.intuition_gloss: Arrange elements into groups so every pair meets equally often — the hidden math behind tournament schedules, sudoku, and error-correcting codes.
concepts.formal_gloss: A t-(v,k,λ) design is a family of k-subsets (blocks) of a v-set in which every t-subset lies in exactly λ blocks; Latin squares are n×n arrays with each symbol once per row and column.
sources: van Lint & Wilson, A Course in Combinatorics

concepts.name: Integer & Combinatorial Optimization
concepts.intuition_gloss: Optimization where the choices snap to whole units — assign this truck or don't, visit this city or skip it — so you can't slide smoothly downhill and must search a huge but finite set of options cleverly.
concepts.formal_gloss: Optimization over discrete feasible sets, canonically integer linear programming (Ax ≤ b, x integral), solved by branch and bound, cutting planes, and LP relaxations whose gap measures the problem's difficulty; many NP-hard problems (TSP, scheduling, knapsack) are combinatorial optimization problems.
sources: Schrijver, Combinatorial Optimization: Polyhedra and Efficiency

concepts.name: Uniform, Exponential & Geometric Distributions
concepts.intuition_gloss: The other stock characters of randomness: the uniform (a spinner where every value is equally likely — the raw material of all simulation), the geometric (coin flips until the first success), and the exponential (the wait for a random arrival, which never remembers how long you've already waited).
concepts.formal_gloss: Uniform(a,b) has constant density 1/(b-a); Geometric(p) has P(X = k) = (1-p)^(k-1) p; Exponential(λ) has density λe^(-λx), is the unique memoryless continuous distribution, and is the interarrival-time law of the Poisson process.
sources: Feller vol. 1 ch. VI and XVII

concepts.name: Compactness & Löwenheim–Skolem Theorems
concepts.intuition_gloss: Two surprises baked into first-order logic: if every finite chunk of a rulebook has a universe satisfying it, the whole rulebook does too — and no such rulebook can control how big its universes are, so countable axioms always fit a countable world and infinite worlds of every larger size.
concepts.formal_gloss: Compactness: a set of first-order sentences has a model iff every finite subset does. Löwenheim–Skolem: a countable first-order theory with an infinite model has models of every infinite cardinality. Together they yield nonstandard models of arithmetic and Skolem's paradox, and they explain why first-order axioms cannot be categorical for infinite structures.

concepts.name: Compass & Straightedge Construction
concepts.intuition_gloss: Drawing exact figures with only a pencil, an unmarked ruler, and a compass — and discovering that some figures are forever out of reach.
concepts.formal_gloss: Figures generable from two given points by finitely many line and circle drawings; the constructible lengths form the closure of Q under square roots, whence trisecting the angle, doubling the cube, and squaring the circle are impossible.
sources: leverage: canonical bridge from geometry to abstract algebra

concepts.name: Computational Geometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: Teaching a computer to see shape: step-by-step recipes that stretch a rubber band around a point cloud, carve a map into nearest-neighbor cells, or mesh a region with well-shaped triangles.
concepts.formal_gloss: The design and analysis of algorithms for geometric input — convex hulls, Voronoi diagrams and Delaunay triangulations, intersection and proximity queries — with running time measured in the size of the point set (e.g., O(n log n) convex hulls).

resources.title: de Berg, Cheong, van Kreveld & Overmars, Computational Geometry: Algorithms and Applications (3rd ed., Springer)

sources: Shamos, Computational Geometry (1978 thesis — founding document)
de Berg, Cheong, van Kreveld & Overmars, Computational Geometry: Algorithms and Applications

concepts.name: Confidence Interval
concepts.intuition_gloss: An honest range around your estimate, built so that the recipe traps the true value a stated fraction of the time.
concepts.formal_gloss: A data-dependent interval [L(X), U(X)] with P(L ≤ θ ≤ U) ≥ 1 - α under repeated sampling, for confidence level 1 - α.

concepts.name: Conic Sections
concepts.intuition_gloss: Slice a cone at different angles and you get circles, ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas — the paths traced by planets and cannonballs.
concepts.formal_gloss: The plane curves defined by degree-2 polynomial equations, classified by type — ellipse, parabola, or hyperbola (plus degenerate cases) — up to affine equivalence.

concepts.name: Constructive vs. Non-constructive Proof
concepts.intuition_gloss: You can prove treasure exists by digging it up, or by showing the map forces it to be somewhere — only the first hands you the treasure.
concepts.formal_gloss: A constructive proof of an existential statement exhibits or algorithmically produces a witness; a non-constructive proof establishes existence without providing one, often via contradiction or counting.
sources: Princeton Companion IV.22 (Logic and Model Theory context)

concepts.name: Continuum Hypothesis & Independence
concepts.intuition_gloss: Is there an infinity strictly between the counting numbers and the number line? Our standard rulebook provably cannot answer — the question is independent of the axioms.
concepts.formal_gloss: The statement that no cardinal lies strictly between |N| and |R| (i.e., 2^ℵ₀ = ℵ₁); Gödel (1940) and Cohen (1963, via forcing) showed CH is neither refutable nor provable from ZFC.

resources.title: The Continuum Hypothesis — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy

concepts.name: Banach Fixed-Point Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Keep applying a map that always shrinks distances and everything spirals into one inevitable fixed point.
concepts.formal_gloss: A contraction on a complete metric space has a unique fixed point, obtained as the limit of iterating the map from any starting point.
sources: Powers Picard existence theorem for ODEs and inverse function theorem — high leverage

concepts.name: Control Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: Steering a system by continuously measuring where it is and nudging it toward where you want it — the mathematics behind thermostats, autopilots, and cruise control.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of dynamical systems with inputs, dx/dt = f(x,u), addressing stabilization, controllability, and observability — canonically via feedback laws u = -Kx placing the eigenvalues of the closed-loop linear system.

resources.title: Brunton & Kutz — Data-Driven Science and Engineering (control chapters + video lectures)

concepts.name: Convex Optimization
concepts.intuition_gloss: In a bowl-shaped landscape there are no false valleys — any local bottom is the global bottom, which is why these problems are the ones we can reliably solve at scale.
concepts.formal_gloss: Minimization of a convex function over a convex feasible set, where every local minimum is global, first-order (KKT) conditions certify optimality, and Lagrangian duality gives computable bounds.

concepts.name: Counterexample
concepts.intuition_gloss: One black swan kills 'all swans are white' — a single well-chosen example demolishes a universal claim for good.
concepts.formal_gloss: An object satisfying the hypotheses of a universally quantified statement but violating its conclusion, thereby disproving the statement.

resources.title: Book of Proof (Hammack), ch. on disproof

sources: Lakatos, Proofs and Refutations (counterexample-driven concept refinement)

concepts.name: Cross Product
concepts.intuition_gloss: From two arrows in 3D, build a third one at right angles to both, whose length is the area of the parallelogram the two trace out.
concepts.formal_gloss: The bilinear operation on R³ with u × v orthogonal to both u and v, |u × v| = |u||v|sin θ, and direction given by the right-hand rule; componentwise it is the formal determinant with rows (i, j, k), u, v.
sources: standard calculus III and intro linear algebra curricula

concepts.name: Cryptography Basics
concepts.intuition_gloss: Locks made of pure mathematics: scramble a message so that undoing the scramble is easy with a secret key but computationally hopeless without it — even when the eavesdropper knows exactly how the lock works.
concepts.formal_gloss: The design of encryption schemes whose security rests on computational hardness: symmetric ciphers share a secret key, while public-key systems (Diffie-Hellman, RSA) let strangers communicate securely because inverting the public operation — e.g. factoring or computing discrete logarithms — is believed intractable.
sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics §VII.7 (Mathematics and Cryptography)
Katz & Lindell, Introduction to Modern Cryptography

concepts.name: Cyclic Group
concepts.intuition_gloss: A group cranked out by one repeating move — like the hour hand's twelve positions — the simplest engine of symmetry there is.
concepts.formal_gloss: A group generated by a single element, isomorphic to Z if infinite or to Z/nZ if of order n; every subgroup of a cyclic group is cyclic.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

concepts.name: Decidability & the Halting Problem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Some yes/no questions no possible program can answer for every input — most famously, whether an arbitrary program will ever finish running.
concepts.formal_gloss: A problem is decidable if a Turing machine computes its answer on every input; the halting problem and first-order validity (Church–Turing, 1936) are undecidable.

resources.title: Turing Machines — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy

sources: Sipser, Introduction to the Theory of Computation

concepts.name: Differential Forms & Exterior Calculus
concepts.intuition_gloss: The things calculus integrates, packaged to be coordinate-free: tiny oriented lengths, areas, and volumes you can add up along curves, over surfaces, and beyond.
concepts.formal_gloss: Antisymmetric multilinear fields (k-forms) equipped with the wedge product and the exterior derivative d; they are the natural integrands over oriented k-dimensional regions, d unifies gradient, curl, and divergence, and the generalized Stokes theorem reads ∫_∂M ω = ∫_M dω.
sources: do Carmo, Differential Forms and Applications
leverage: the floor under Stokes' theorem, de Rham cohomology, and integration on manifolds

concepts.name: Divide and Conquer
concepts.intuition_gloss: Split a big problem into a few smaller copies of itself, solve those the same way, and stitch the answers back together — the recursion does the heavy lifting.
concepts.formal_gloss: An algorithm design paradigm that recursively partitions a problem into subproblems of smaller size, solves them independently, and combines the results, with running time governed by recurrences such as T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) analyzed via the master theorem.

concepts.name: Dynamic Programming
concepts.intuition_gloss: Solve a big problem by solving each small subproblem exactly once, writing the answers in a table, and never repeating work.
concepts.formal_gloss: An algorithmic technique for problems with optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems, expressing the optimal value via a Bellman recurrence and computing it bottom-up or with memoization.

resources.title: MIT OCW 6.006 Introduction to Algorithms (dynamic programming lectures)

concepts.name: Ergodic Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: For thoroughly mixing systems, following one trajectory long enough tells you the same story as sampling everywhere at once.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of measure-preserving transformations; Birkhoff's theorem states that for ergodic systems, time averages along almost every orbit equal the space average against the invariant measure.
sources: Walters, An Introduction to Ergodic Theory

concepts.name: Erlangen Program (Geometry via Symmetry Groups)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Klein's big idea: a geometry is defined not by its objects but by the moves you are allowed to make — study whatever the moves leave unchanged.
concepts.formal_gloss: Klein's 1872 organizing principle classifying geometries as the study of invariants of a transformation group acting on a space, ordering Euclidean ⊂ affine ⊂ projective by group inclusion.
sources: Klein, Vergleichende Betrachtungen über neuere geometrische Forschungen (1872)
leverage: the single deepest 'what is geometry?' answer; template for modern invariant-based thinking

concepts.name: Error-Correcting Codes
concepts.intuition_gloss: Sprinkle carefully chosen redundancy into a message so that even after noise flips some bits, the receiver can spot the damage and repair it — how CDs survive scratches and probes phone home from deep space.
concepts.formal_gloss: Subsets of strings (codes) whose elements are pairwise far apart in Hamming distance, so that a code of minimum distance d corrects up to (d-1)/2 errors; linear codes (Hamming, Reed-Solomon, LDPC) achieve this via linear algebra over finite fields, approaching the channel capacity promised by Shannon's noisy-channel theorem.
sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics §VII.6 (Reliable Transmission of Information)
MacWilliams & Sloane, The Theory of Error-Correcting Codes

concepts.name: Estimation & Sanity Checking
concepts.intuition_gloss: Before trusting an answer, weigh it on rough scales — check units, extreme cases, and orders of magnitude to catch nonsense early.
concepts.formal_gloss: The practice of bounding a quantity by decomposing it into estimable factors and testing results against limiting cases, dimensional constraints, and known benchmarks.

concepts.name: Eulerian Circuits & Hamiltonian Cycles
concepts.intuition_gloss: Two famous tours of a network — trace every bridge exactly once (easy to check), or visit every city exactly once (famously hard).
concepts.formal_gloss: A connected graph has an Eulerian circuit iff every vertex has even degree; deciding Hamiltonicity (a cycle through every vertex) is NP-complete, with only sufficient conditions like Dirac's theorem known.
sources: Euler 1736 (Königsberg — the founding problem of graph theory)

concepts.name: Experimental Design & Causal Inference
concepts.intuition_gloss: Randomly splitting subjects into treated and untreated groups so that any systematic difference in outcomes can only be blamed on the treatment.
concepts.formal_gloss: The design of data collection — randomization, control, replication, blocking — that licenses causal rather than merely associational conclusions; correlation alone does not identify causation.
sources: Freedman, Pisani & Purves, Statistics part I

concepts.name: Exploiting Symmetry
concepts.intuition_gloss: When a problem looks the same after a swap, rotation, or relabeling, that sameness is free information — use it to halve the work or force equalities; this is the discovery-side strategy whose proof-writing counterpart is WLOG.
concepts.formal_gloss: The strategy of using a problem's automorphisms — transformations preserving its data — to reduce cases, constrain solutions, or average over orbits.
sources: Polya, How to Solve It (symmetry heuristic)

concepts.name: Exponentials & Logarithms
concepts.intuition_gloss: Repeated multiplication grown up into a smooth machine for explosive growth, and its inverse, which converts multiplication into addition.
concepts.formal_gloss: The functions b^x, extended from integer exponents to the reals by the laws b^(x+y) = b^x b^y, and log_b as their inverse, satisfying log(xy) = log x + log y.

concepts.name: The Exponential Function & e
concepts.intuition_gloss: The function that grows at a rate equal to its own current size — and e is the base that makes the match exact: one unit of growth per unit already there.
concepts.formal_gloss: exp(x) = Σ x^n/n! = lim (1 + x/n)^n, the unique solution of f' = f with f(0) = 1; e = exp(1), and on C, e^{iθ} = cos θ + i sin θ (Euler's formula).

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: What's so special about Euler's number e?

sources: Rudin Ch. 8 (the exponential and logarithmic functions)

concepts.name: Exponential Growth & Decay
concepts.intuition_gloss: When a quantity grows or shrinks in proportion to its current size — compound interest, bacteria, radioactive decay — it traces the exponential curve.
concepts.formal_gloss: The solution y(t) = y_0 e^{kt} of the linear ODE dy/dt = ky; for y_0 ≠ 0 it grows without bound in magnitude when k > 0 and decays to zero when k < 0.
sources: AP Calculus / MIT OCW 18.03 opening unit
Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, ch. 2 (flows on the line)

concepts.name: Extremal Combinatorics
concepts.intuition_gloss: How big can a structure get before it's forced to contain a forbidden pattern — how many wires before a triangle must appear?
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of maximum or minimum sizes of set systems or graphs avoiding a given substructure; Turán's theorem gives the maximum edges in a K_r-free graph, with Szemerédi's regularity lemma as the deep modern tool.

resources.title: Diestel, Graph Theory, ch. 7 (free online edition)

concepts.name: Extremal Principle
concepts.intuition_gloss: Grab the biggest, smallest, or closest object in the problem — extremes are cornered animals, and their special position forces conclusions.
concepts.formal_gloss: The heuristic of examining an element that is extremal with respect to some ordering, whose maximality or minimality yields constraints, often combined with well-ordering or contradiction.

resources.title: Problem-Solving Strategies (Engel), ch. 3: The Extremal Principle

concepts.name: Fast Fourier Transform
concepts.intuition_gloss: A divide-and-conquer trick that decomposes a signal into its constituent frequencies thousands of times faster than the obvious way — arguably the most-run algorithm on Earth.
concepts.formal_gloss: An O(n log n) algorithm computing the discrete Fourier transform (evaluation of a polynomial at the n-th roots of unity) by recursively splitting into even and odd indices.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — But what is the Fourier Transform?

sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics §III.26 (The Fast Fourier Transform)

concepts.name: Fermat's Last Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: No cube splits into two cubes, and likewise for every higher power — a margin note that took 358 years and a whole new bridge of mathematics to prove.
concepts.formal_gloss: For n >= 3, x^n + y^n = z^n has no positive integer solutions; proved by Wiles (1995) via the modularity of semistable elliptic curves.

resources.title: Singh, Fermat's Last Theorem (popular account of the Wiles proof)

sources: Princeton Companion V — theorem as landmark
statement is elementary; placement reflects its role connecting territories

concepts.name: Fiber Bundle
concepts.intuition_gloss: A space built by attaching a copy of the same fiber over every point of a base — like the hairs on a scalp — where the global twisting (as in the Möbius band over the circle) is the interesting part.
concepts.formal_gloss: A map E → B locally trivial in the sense that every point of B has a neighborhood U with E over U homeomorphic to U × F respecting projection; global twisting is measured by characteristic classes.
sources: Princeton Companion IV (differential topology / gauge theory connections)

concepts.name: Fourier Series & Transform
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any signal, however jagged, is secretly a sum of pure sine waves — Fourier analysis is the prism that splits it into them.
concepts.formal_gloss: Decomposition of functions into orthogonal trigonometric (or complex exponential) components: series Σ c_n e^{inx} on periodic domains, and the integral transform f̂(ξ) = ∫ f(x)e^{-2πixξ}dx on R.

resources.title: Stein & Shakarchi, Fourier Analysis: An Introduction

concepts.name: Fractal Geometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: Shapes that stay rough at every zoom level — coastlines and snowflakes whose detail never bottoms out, with dimensions that are not whole numbers.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of sets exhibiting self-similarity and non-integer Hausdorff dimension, often arising as attractors of iterated function systems.
sources: Mandelbrot, The Fractal Geometry of Nature

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra
concepts.intuition_gloss: Once you allow numbers in the plane, no polynomial equation is unsolvable — every one of degree n ≥ 1 has exactly n roots, counted with repeats.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every nonconstant polynomial with complex coefficients has a root in C; equivalently, C is algebraically closed, so every degree-n complex polynomial splits into n linear factors.

resources.title: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra — Numberphile (David Eisenbud)

sources: Princeton Companion V.13 (The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra)

concepts.name: Galois Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: Attach to each equation the group of ways its roots can be shuffled without breaking any algebraic relation — and read off from that group's shape why the quintic has no formula.
concepts.formal_gloss: The correspondence between intermediate fields of a Galois extension and subgroups of its automorphism group; a polynomial is solvable by radicals iff its Galois group is a solvable group.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

sources: Princeton Companion V.21 (insolubility of the quintic)

concepts.name: Game Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: The mathematics of strategy when your best move depends on what everyone else does — and the stable standoffs where nobody gains by deviating alone.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of strategic interaction among rational players, centered on the Nash equilibrium — a strategy profile where each player's strategy is a best response to the others' — which exists in mixed strategies for every finite game.

resources.title: Yale Open Courses — ECON 159 Game Theory (Ben Polak)

sources: von Neumann & Morgenstern, Theory of Games and Economic Behavior
Princeton Companion to Mathematics §VII.8 (Mathematics and Economic Reasoning)

concepts.name: Gauss–Bonnet Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Add up all the bending over a closed surface and the answer ignores every dent and bulge — it is fixed by the number of holes alone.
concepts.formal_gloss: For a compact surface S without boundary, ∫_S K dA = 2πχ(S): total Gaussian curvature equals 2π times the Euler characteristic; for regions with boundary, geodesic curvature and exterior angles join the tally.
sources: Gauss, Disquisitiones generales circa superficies curvas (1827)
do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces, ch. 4
leverage: the crown-jewel bridge from local curvature to global topology

concepts.name: Generalization & Abstraction
concepts.intuition_gloss: Strip away what's incidental until different problems reveal the same skeleton — then solve the skeleton once, everywhere.
concepts.formal_gloss: The process of extracting the essential structure common to specific instances into a general definition or axiom system whose theorems apply to every instance at once.

resources.title: The Princeton Companion to Mathematics — I.4 The General Goals of Mathematical Research

sources: history of the axiomatic method (Hilbert, Bourbaki)

concepts.name: Generating Functions
concepts.intuition_gloss: A clothesline that hangs an entire infinite sequence on a single algebraic expression, so multiplying expressions does the counting for you.
concepts.formal_gloss: The formal power series A(x) = sum of a_n x^n encoding a sequence (a_n); products and functional operations on series correspond to combinatorial constructions on the counted objects.
sources: Wilf, generatingfunctionology ('a clothesline on which we hang up a sequence')

concepts.name: Geometry of Numbers
concepts.intuition_gloss: Draw a symmetric blob around the origin of a grid; make it big enough and it must swallow a grid point — a purely geometric squeeze that forces number-theoretic conclusions.
concepts.formal_gloss: Minkowski's theorem: a convex, centrally symmetric region of volume > 2^n det(L) contains a nonzero point of the lattice L; the method yields finiteness of the class number, Dirichlet's unit theorem, and the four-square theorem (MSC 11H).

resources.title: Cassels, An Introduction to the Geometry of Numbers (Springer)

sources: bridge between convex geometry and algebraic number theory, MSC 11H

concepts.name: Gödel's Incompleteness Theorems
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any rulebook rich enough for arithmetic contains true statements it can never prove — including, fatally, its own consistency.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every consistent, effectively axiomatized theory extending basic arithmetic is incomplete (some sentence is neither provable nor refutable), and no such theory proves its own consistency.

resources.title: Gödel's Incompleteness Theorems — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy

concepts.name: Greedy Algorithms
concepts.intuition_gloss: Always grab the option that looks best right now and never look back — surprisingly, for some problems this shortsighted strategy is provably perfect.
concepts.formal_gloss: An algorithm design paradigm making the locally optimal choice at each step, correct exactly when the problem has the greedy-choice property (provable by exchange arguments, and characterized in general by matroid structure) — as in Dijkstra, Kruskal, Prim, and Huffman coding.

concepts.name: Representation Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: Turn every abstract symmetry into a concrete matrix, so the whole machinery of linear algebra can X-ray a group's internal anatomy.
concepts.formal_gloss: A homomorphism from a group to the invertible linear maps of a vector space; over C every finite-group representation decomposes into irreducibles, which characters classify completely.

resources.title: Introduction to Representation Theory (Etingof et al., MIT notes)

concepts.name: Hamiltonian & Conservative Systems
concepts.intuition_gloss: Frictionless physics as geometry: energy is a landscape, and motion follows its contour lines, never compressing the cloud of possible states.
concepts.formal_gloss: Dynamics q' = ∂H/∂p, p' = −∂H/∂q generated by a Hamiltonian function H; the flow conserves H and preserves phase-space volume (Liouville's theorem).

resources.title: Susskind — The Theoretical Minimum: Classical Mechanics

sources: Arnold, Mathematical Methods of Classical Mechanics

concepts.name: Hausdorff Space & Separation Axioms
concepts.intuition_gloss: A space where any two distinct points can be fenced off from each other in their own non-overlapping neighborhoods, so points can't blur together.
concepts.formal_gloss: A topological space in which any two distinct points have disjoint open neighborhoods; the T2 condition in the hierarchy of separation axioms (T0 through T4 and beyond). Every metric space is Hausdorff, and metrization theorems (e.g., Urysohn's) characterize when a topology comes from a metric.

concepts.name: Heat Equation (Diffusion)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Sharp temperature differences smooth themselves out — each point drifts toward the average of its neighbors.
concepts.formal_gloss: The parabolic PDE u_t = α∇²u, solved on bounded domains by separation of variables and Fourier series, with instant smoothing and maximum-principle behavior.
sources: Fourier's Théorie analytique de la chaleur (historical root)

concepts.name: Hilbert Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: An infinite-dimensional space that still has angles and perpendicularity, so geometry's intuition — projections, Pythagoras — works on functions.
concepts.formal_gloss: A complete inner product space; every element expands uniquely against an orthonormal basis, generalizing Euclidean geometry to infinitely many dimensions.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.102 Introduction to Functional Analysis

sources: Mathematical foundation of quantum mechanics — high leverage

concepts.name: Homological Algebra
concepts.intuition_gloss: Chains of maps where doing two steps in a row always gives zero; homology measures the leftover slack — things that die at the next step but didn't come from the previous one — and that slack detects the hidden holes and obstructions in an algebraic situation.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of chain complexes and exact sequences of modules, extracting invariants like homology, Ext, and Tor via derived functors in an abelian category.
sources: Princeton Companion (algebraic topology & modern algebra essays, IV.6)
Weibel, An Introduction to Homological Algebra (reference placement)

concepts.name: Homology
concepts.intuition_gloss: A systematic hole-detector: it counts the independent loops, cavities, and higher-dimensional voids of a space by tracking which cycles fail to be boundaries.
concepts.formal_gloss: The sequence of abelian groups H_n(X) = ker ∂_n / im ∂_{n+1} of a chain complex built from the space; their ranks are the Betti numbers, and homology is a functorial homotopy invariant. Its dual theory, cohomology, carries an additional ring structure.

concepts.name: Hypothesis Testing
concepts.intuition_gloss: Assume the boring explanation, compute how surprising your data would be under it, and reject the boring story only if the surprise is extreme.
concepts.formal_gloss: A decision rule for a null hypothesis H₀ versus alternative H₁ controlling Type I error at level α; the p-value is the probability, under H₀, of data at least as extreme as observed.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

concepts.name: Inverse & Implicit Function Theorems
concepts.intuition_gloss: The circle x² + y² = 1 is no function's graph — yet zoom in anywhere but its two side edges and the little arc you see is one. Wherever an equation's constraint surface isn't vertical, it secretly is a graph, locally.
concepts.formal_gloss: A C^1 map with invertible Jacobian at a point is a local diffeomorphism; correspondingly, F(x,y) = 0 with invertible ∂F/∂y locally defines y as a C^1 function of x.

resources.title: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch. 9

sources: Prerequisite for manifold theory (Lean mathlib dependency structure)

concepts.name: Inclusion–Exclusion Principle
concepts.intuition_gloss: To count things in overlapping groups, add the groups up, subtract the double-counted overlaps, add back the triple overlaps, and so on.
concepts.formal_gloss: |A_1 ∪ ... ∪ A_n| equals the alternating sum over nonempty index subsets S of (-1)^(|S|+1) |intersection of A_i for i in S|.

resources.title: MIT OCW 6.042J Mathematics for Computer Science

sources: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications ch. 8

concepts.name: Inequality
concepts.intuition_gloss: An equation's looser cousin: instead of pinning a value down exactly, it fences off a whole region where the answer must live.
concepts.formal_gloss: A relation asserting one expression is less than (or at most) another under the order on the reals; solution sets are intervals or regions, and multiplying by negatives reverses the relation.

concepts.name: Integer Partitions & Compositions
concepts.intuition_gloss: The ways to break a whole number into a sum of smaller pieces — drawn as staircases of dots that reveal surprising symmetries.
concepts.formal_gloss: A partition of n is a weakly decreasing sequence of positive integers summing to n; p(n) counts them, with Euler's product formula giving the generating function 1/product(1-x^k).

resources.title: Andrews & Eriksson, Integer Partitions (Cambridge University Press)

sources: canonical meeting point of combinatorics and number theory (Euler, Ramanujan)

concepts.name: Intermediate Value Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: A continuous path from below a level to above it must cross that level somewhere — you can't get across a river without touching the water.
concepts.formal_gloss: If f is continuous on [a,b] and y lies between f(a) and f(b), then f(c) = y for some c in [a,b]; a consequence of the connectedness of intervals.

concepts.name: Invariants & Monovariants
concepts.intuition_gloss: Find something the allowed moves can never change — if the goal state has a different value of it, the goal is provably unreachable.
concepts.formal_gloss: A quantity or property preserved (invariant) or monotonically changed (monovariant) by every permitted operation, used to prove impossibility or termination.

resources.title: Problem-Solving Strategies (Engel), ch. 1: The Invariance Principle

concepts.name: Inverse Problems & Regularization
concepts.intuition_gloss: Reasoning from effects back to causes — reconstructing the inside of a body from X-ray shadows — where naive inversion amplifies noise and must be tamed by penalizing wild answers.
concepts.formal_gloss: Estimating model parameters m from indirect observations d = G(m) + noise when the forward map is ill-conditioned or non-injective, stabilized by regularization such as minimizing ||G(m)-d||² + λ||m||².
sources: Engl, Hanke & Neubauer, Regularization of Inverse Problems

concepts.name: Irrationality & Transcendence
concepts.intuition_gloss: Some numbers, like √2, can never be written as fractions — and some, like π and e, can't even be captured by any polynomial equation.
concepts.formal_gloss: A real number is irrational if it lies outside Q, and transcendental if it is a root of no nonzero polynomial with rational coefficients; algebraic numbers are countable, so almost all reals are transcendental.

resources.title: Square root of 2 is irrational — Wikipedia

sources: the √2 proof is the canonical first contradiction proof

concepts.name: Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
concepts.intuition_gloss: When a linear system is too enormous to solve head-on, start with a guess and repeatedly nudge it toward the answer, stopping when it's close enough.
concepts.formal_gloss: Methods solving Ax = b by generating a convergent sequence of approximations — classically via matrix splittings (Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel) or Krylov subspaces (conjugate gradient, GMRES) — with convergence governed by the spectrum of A.

resources.title: Shewchuk — An Introduction to the Conjugate Gradient Method Without the Agonizing Pain

concepts.name: Jordan Curve Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any loop drawn in the plane without crossing itself has an inside and an outside — obvious to the eye, famously slippery to prove.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every simple closed curve in the plane separates it into exactly two connected components, one bounded and one unbounded, with the curve as their common boundary. (The stronger Jordan-Schoenflies theorem — that the bounded region is a disk — is a distinct result that fails in higher dimensions.)
sources: standard example of intuition vs. rigor gap (formalized early in proof assistants)

concepts.name: Jordan Normal Form
concepts.intuition_gloss: The honest portrait of a transformation that can't be fully diagonalized — stretches plus unavoidable little shears, arranged in blocks.
concepts.formal_gloss: Over an algebraically closed field, every square matrix is similar to a unique (up to order) block-diagonal matrix of Jordan blocks, classifying operators up to similarity.

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 8

concepts.name: Knot Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: Take a tangled loop of string with the ends fused and ask: is it truly knotted, or can wiggling untie it — and how do you prove two tangles are different?
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of embeddings of the circle in 3-space up to ambient isotopy, with invariants such as tricolorability, the knot group, and polynomial invariants (Alexander, Jones) distinguishing knot types.
sources: Adams 'The Knot Book' as standard accessible text

concepts.name: Laplace's Equation & Harmonic Functions
concepts.intuition_gloss: The equation of perfect balance — a stretched soap film or a temperature map that has stopped changing, where every point sits exactly at the average of its neighbors.
concepts.formal_gloss: The elliptic PDE ∇²u = 0; its solutions, harmonic functions, satisfy the mean value property and the maximum principle, describe steady states of the heat equation, and model electrostatic and gravitational potentials.

concepts.name: Laplace Transform
concepts.intuition_gloss: Feed the whole time-history of a signal through a prism that turns calculus into algebra — derivatives become multiplications by s, you solve with ordinary arithmetic, then translate back.
concepts.formal_gloss: The integral transform F(s) = ∫_0^∞ f(t) e^{-st} dt, which converts linear constant-coefficient ODEs with initial conditions into algebraic equations in s; the poles of the transformed solution govern stability and response, making it the standard engineering route to transfer functions.

concepts.name: Lebesgue Integral
concepts.intuition_gloss: Instead of slicing the ground under a curve into vertical strips, slice by height and ask how much ground sits at each level — an integral that survives limits gracefully.
concepts.formal_gloss: The integral built from measures of level sets of measurable functions, extending the Riemann integral and satisfying the monotone and dominated convergence theorems.

resources.title: Terence Tao, An Introduction to Measure Theory

sources: Rudin, Real and Complex Analysis Ch. 1

concepts.name: Lie Groups & Lie Algebras
concepts.intuition_gloss: Symmetries that vary smoothly, like all rotations of a sphere — studied by zooming in on the infinitesimal nudges near 'do nothing', which form an algebra of their own.
concepts.formal_gloss: A Lie group is a smooth manifold with smooth group operations; its tangent space at the identity carries a bilinear bracket [x,y] satisfying antisymmetry and the Jacobi identity, forming its Lie algebra.

concepts.name: Limit Cycle
concepts.intuition_gloss: An isolated closed loop the system settles into — the mathematical heartbeat behind clocks, neurons, and predator-prey cycles.
concepts.formal_gloss: An isolated closed orbit of a flow; in the plane, the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem guarantees a closed orbit (not necessarily isolated) inside any trapping region containing no equilibria.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Regression & Least Squares
concepts.intuition_gloss: Draw the straight line through a cloud of points that misses them least, then use it to predict one quantity from another.
concepts.formal_gloss: Modeling E[Y|X] = β₀ + β₁X (or Xβ in matrix form) with coefficients chosen to minimize the sum of squared residuals; geometrically, an orthogonal projection onto the column space of X.

resources.title: James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani — An Introduction to Statistical Learning

concepts.name: Logistic Map & Period Doubling
concepts.intuition_gloss: A one-line population rule that, as you turn up the growth knob, goes from calm to oscillating to full-blown chaos — the fruit fly of chaos theory.
concepts.formal_gloss: The map x_{n+1} = r x_n(1 − x_n) on [0,1], whose attracting cycles undergo a period-doubling cascade to chaos governed by the universal Feigenbaum constant δ ≈ 4.669.

resources.title: Veritasium — This equation will change how you see the world (the logistic map)

concepts.name: Lyapunov Exponent
concepts.intuition_gloss: The speedometer of chaos — how fast two almost-identical starting points fly apart, their gap stretching by a fixed factor with every tick of the clock.
concepts.formal_gloss: The asymptotic exponential growth rate λ = lim_{t→∞} (1/t) ln(|δx(t)|/|δx(0)|) of infinitesimal perturbations; a positive largest exponent is the standard quantitative signature of chaos.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Machine Learning & Statistical Learning
concepts.intuition_gloss: Instead of programming the rules, show the machine examples and let it fit a function that predicts well on cases it has never seen.
concepts.formal_gloss: The problem of choosing, from a hypothesis class, a function minimizing expected loss on an unknown data distribution given a finite sample, balancing fit against complexity (bias-variance trade-off, regularization, generalization bounds).

resources.title: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman — The Elements of Statistical Learning (free book)
Brunton & Kutz — Data-Driven Science and Engineering (book + video lectures)

sources: Hastie et al., Elements of Statistical Learning

concepts.name: Markov Chain
concepts.intuition_gloss: A memoryless random walk between states: where you go next depends only on where you stand now, not how you got there.
concepts.formal_gloss: A stochastic process with P(X_{n+1} = j | X_n = i, past) = P(X_{n+1} = j | X_n = i), encoded by a transition matrix whose long-run behavior is governed by its stationary distribution.
sources: Ross, Introduction to Probability Models ch. 4

concepts.name: Martingale
concepts.intuition_gloss: A fair game over time: given everything that's happened so far, your expected fortune tomorrow equals your fortune today.
concepts.formal_gloss: A process {X_n} adapted to a filtration with E[|X_n|] < ∞ and E[X_{n+1} | F_n] = X_n; optional stopping and convergence theorems make it a central proof tool of modern probability.
sources: Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples ch. 4

concepts.name: Matchings & Hall's Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Pair up two groups — applicants to jobs, say — so nobody is doubled up; Hall's condition tells you exactly when everyone can be matched.
concepts.formal_gloss: A matching is a set of pairwise non-adjacent edges; Hall's theorem states a bipartite graph has a matching saturating side A iff every subset S of A has at least |S| neighbors.

resources.title: Diestel, Graph Theory, ch. 2 (free online edition)

sources: König's theorem / LP duality connection — Princeton Companion combinatorics chapters

concepts.name: Mathematical Notation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Good notation is a lever: it packs an idea into marks that think for you, the way positional digits made arithmetic easy.
concepts.formal_gloss: The system of conventional symbols and syntax for denoting mathematical objects and operations, whose design (compactness, suggestiveness, unambiguity) materially affects reasoning.
