sources: Princeton Companion I.2 (The Language and Grammar of Mathematics)

concepts.name: Matrix Factorizations (LU, QR, Cholesky)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Take a matrix apart into simple triangular or rotational pieces, the way you'd factor a number into primes to compute with it faster.
concepts.formal_gloss: Standard decompositions A = LU (elimination), A = QR (orthogonalization), A = LLᵀ (Cholesky, for positive definite A) underpinning practical solution of linear systems.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.06, Lecture 4: Factorization into A = LU

concepts.name: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Pick the parameter value under which the data you actually saw would have been least surprising.
concepts.formal_gloss: θ̂_MLE = argmax_θ L(θ; x) where L is the joint density of the data as a function of θ; under regularity conditions MLEs are consistent, asymptotically normal, and efficient.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

concepts.name: Metacognitive Monitoring
concepts.intuition_gloss: The inner supervisor who asks 'is this approach working? do I actually understand this step?' — expert problem solvers audit themselves constantly.
concepts.formal_gloss: The executive-control component of problem solving — planning, progress monitoring, and strategy switching — identified by Schoenfeld as a chief determinant of problem-solving success beyond resource knowledge.

resources.title: How to Solve It (Polya) — Wikipedia ('looking back')

concepts.name: Möbius Band & One-Sidedness
concepts.intuition_gloss: Tape a paper strip into a loop with a half twist and you get a surface with only one side and one edge — an ant walking its length comes back on the 'other' side without ever crossing an edge.
concepts.formal_gloss: The surface obtained from a rectangle by identifying one pair of opposite edges with a flip; it is non-orientable, has a single boundary circle, and cutting it along the center line yields one longer two-sided band rather than two pieces.
sources: Möbius and Listing (1858) as classical origin
standard hands-on entry point in popular and school treatments of topology

concepts.name: Modular Forms
concepts.intuition_gloss: Functions with an outrageous amount of symmetry — so much that only a rigid, precious few survive — whose coefficient lists mysteriously encode deep arithmetic; Eichler called them the 'fifth operation of arithmetic'.
concepts.formal_gloss: Holomorphic functions f on the upper half-plane satisfying f((az+b)/(cz+d)) = (cz+d)^k f(z) for matrices in a congruence subgroup of SL2(Z), plus growth conditions; the modularity theorem attaches one to every rational elliptic curve.

resources.title: Diamond & Shurman, A First Course in Modular Forms

concepts.name: Moore–Penrose Pseudoinverse
concepts.intuition_gloss: The best stand-in for an inverse when a true one doesn't exist — undo what the matrix did wherever possible, and land on the closest answer where it isn't.
concepts.formal_gloss: The unique matrix A⁺ satisfying the four Penrose conditions; computed from the SVD as A⁺ = VΣ⁺Uᵀ, it yields the minimum-norm least-squares solution x = A⁺b of Ax = b.

concepts.name: Network Flows & Max-Flow Min-Cut
concepts.intuition_gloss: Push as much water as possible through a network of pipes — the bottleneck is always the tightest cut separating source from sink.
concepts.formal_gloss: In a capacitated directed graph, the maximum value of a source-to-sink flow equals the minimum capacity of an edge cut separating them (Ford-Fulkerson, 1956).

resources.title: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest & Stein, Introduction to Algorithms, 4th ed., flow networks chapter (MIT Press)

sources: CLRS ch. 24 (flows; 4th ed. — ch. 26 in 3rd ed.)
unifies Menger, König, Hall — high structural leverage

concepts.name: Neural Networks & Backpropagation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Stack many layers of simple weighted sums and squashes, then tune millions of knobs by passing error signals backwards — a trainable universal function machine.
concepts.formal_gloss: Compositions of affine maps and nonlinear activations forming parametric function approximators, dense in continuous functions on compacta (universal approximation), trained by gradient descent with gradients computed via the chain rule (backpropagation).

resources.title: Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville — Deep Learning (free book)

concepts.name: Non-Euclidean Geometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: Perfectly consistent worlds where parallel lines can meet or fan apart, and triangle angles refuse to sum to 180 degrees.
concepts.formal_gloss: Geometries satisfying Euclid's axioms except the parallel postulate: elliptic/spherical (no parallels) and hyperbolic (infinitely many), realized on surfaces of constant positive or negative curvature.

resources.title: How One Line in the Oldest Math Text Hinted at Hidden Universes (Veritasium)

sources: leverage: historic proof that axioms are choices, feeding into modern logic and Riemannian geometry

concepts.name: Numerical Integration
concepts.intuition_gloss: Estimate an area you can't compute exactly by sampling the curve at a few well-chosen points and taking a clever weighted sum.
concepts.formal_gloss: Approximating a definite integral by a weighted sum of function values at nodes, with rules (Newton-Cotes, Gaussian quadrature) chosen so the formula is exact on polynomials up to a given degree.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.335J Introduction to Numerical Methods

sources: standard numerical analysis curricula (Burden & Faires ch. 4)

concepts.name: Numerical PDE Methods
concepts.intuition_gloss: Simulate heat spreading, fluids flowing, or structures bending by carving space into a mesh of tiny cells and enforcing the physics on each cell.
concepts.formal_gloss: Discretizations of partial differential equations — finite differences on grids, finite elements via weak forms on meshes, spectral expansions in global bases — reducing the PDE to a large sparse algebraic system.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.085 Computational Science and Engineering I (Strang)

sources: Strang & Fix, An Analysis of the Finite Element Method

concepts.name: Numerical Solution of ODEs
concepts.intuition_gloss: When formulas fail, take tiny time-steps: nudge the state along the arrows over and over and let a computer trace the trajectory.
concepts.formal_gloss: Discrete-time approximation schemes x_{n+1} = x_n + h·Φ(x_n, h) for initial value problems, from first-order Euler to the fourth-order Runge–Kutta family, with accuracy and stability governed by the step size and the method's order.

concepts.name: p-adic Numbers
concepts.intuition_gloss: A parallel universe of numbers where two integers are 'close' when their difference is divisible by a high power of p — completing the rationals in a totally different direction from the reals.
concepts.formal_gloss: Q_p is the completion of Q under the p-adic absolute value |x|_p = p^(-v_p(x)); Ostrowski's theorem says R and the Q_p are the only completions of Q, and Hensel's lemma lifts solutions mod p to Z_p.

concepts.name: P vs NP & NP-Completeness
concepts.intuition_gloss: The million-dollar question: if you can quickly check a solved puzzle, can you always quickly solve it from scratch?
concepts.formal_gloss: The open question whether every problem whose solutions are verifiable in polynomial time (NP) is also solvable in polynomial time (P); NP-complete problems are the hardest in NP, all polynomial-time reducible to one another.

resources.title: Clay Mathematics Institute — P vs NP Millennium Problem

sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics §IV.20 and §V.24 (The P versus NP Problem)

concepts.name: Pell's Equation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Find one magic pair of whole numbers and a simple combining rule climbs from it forever — an endless ladder of ever-larger solutions, every rung built from the single fundamental step below it.
concepts.formal_gloss: The equation x^2 - Dy^2 = 1 for nonsquare D, whose solutions form an infinite cyclic group generated by the fundamental solution, found via the continued fraction of sqrt(D).
sources: units of real quadratic fields — bridge to Dirichlet's unit theorem

concepts.name: Planar Graphs & Euler's Formula
concepts.intuition_gloss: Networks you can draw on paper without wires crossing — and the magic count vertices minus edges plus faces always equals two.
concepts.formal_gloss: A graph embeddable in the plane with no edge crossings; connected planar graphs satisfy V - E + F = 2, and Kuratowski's theorem characterizes planarity by forbidden K5 and K3,3 subdivisions.

resources.title: Diestel, Graph Theory, ch. 4 (free online edition)

concepts.name: Poincaré Conjecture & 3-Manifolds
concepts.intuition_gloss: If every lasso thrown in a closed three-dimensional universe can be reeled all the way in, that universe must be the 3-sphere — a century-old question about the shape of space, finally settled by letting the space flow like heat until its geometry evens out.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every closed, simply connected 3-manifold is homeomorphic to the 3-sphere. Conjectured by Poincaré in 1904 and proved by Perelman (2003) via Hamilton's Ricci flow with surgery, as a special case of Thurston's geometrization of 3-manifolds; it is the only solved Millennium Prize problem.
sources: Perelman's 2002-2003 arXiv papers (via Hamilton's Ricci flow program)

concepts.name: Poincaré Map
concepts.intuition_gloss: Instead of watching the whole orbit, strobe-light it each time it crosses a chosen screen — turning a continuous tangle into a simple sequence of dots.
concepts.formal_gloss: The first-return map induced by a flow on a transversal section, converting a continuous system into a discrete map one dimension lower; periodic orbits of the flow become fixed points of the map.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

sources: Poincaré's three-body work (historical root of the field)

concepts.name: Poisson Distribution & Process
concepts.intuition_gloss: The mathematics of random arrivals — raindrops, emails, radioactive decays — when events strike independently at a steady average rate.
concepts.formal_gloss: The Poisson(λ) distribution P(X = k) = e^(-λ)λ^k/k! arises as the limit of binomials with np → λ; the Poisson process is a counting process with independent, stationary increments of this law.

resources.title: Random (Virtual Laboratories in Probability) — Poisson process

concepts.name: Counting under Symmetry (Burnside & Pólya)
concepts.intuition_gloss: How many truly different necklaces can you bead, when rotating one doesn't make it new? Average the counts fixed by each symmetry.
concepts.formal_gloss: The number of orbits of a finite group G acting on a set equals the average number of fixed points of its elements; Pólya's theorem refines this with cycle-index generating functions.
sources: van Lint & Wilson, A Course in Combinatorics ch. 35
the canonical bridge from group theory into counting

concepts.name: Population & Interaction Models
concepts.intuition_gloss: Simple rules — grow, crowd, hunt, infect — turned into equations that reproduce booms, crashes, cycles, and epidemic waves.
concepts.formal_gloss: Nonlinear ODE models such as logistic growth y' = ry(1 − y/K), the Lotka–Volterra predator-prey system, and SIR compartmental models, analyzed through their equilibria and phase portraits.

concepts.name: Principal Component Analysis
concepts.intuition_gloss: Rotate your cloud of data points until you're looking at it from the angle that shows the most spread — the few directions that matter most — and flatten away the rest.
concepts.formal_gloss: The orthogonal change of basis diagonalizing a dataset's covariance matrix, ordering directions (principal components) by variance explained; computed via the eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix or, equivalently and more stably, the singular value decomposition of the centered data matrix.

resources.title: Setosa — Principal Component Analysis explained visually

sources: Hastie et al., Elements of Statistical Learning §14.5

concepts.name: Reduction to a Known Problem
concepts.intuition_gloss: The mathematician's oldest trick: don't solve the problem in front of you — turn it into one somebody already solved.
concepts.formal_gloss: Solving problem A by exhibiting a transformation carrying instances of A to instances of an already-solved problem B and mapping B's solutions back to A.

resources.title: Reduction (complexity) — Wikipedia (the formal analogue: transforming one problem into another)

sources: Polya, How to Solve It (auxiliary problems)

concepts.name: Proof by Contrapositive
concepts.intuition_gloss: Instead of proving 'if it rains, the ground gets wet', prove 'if the ground is dry, it didn't rain' — the same claim attacked from the other end.
concepts.formal_gloss: A proof of P implies Q obtained by directly proving the logically equivalent statement not-Q implies not-P.

concepts.name: Formalization & Proof Assistants
concepts.intuition_gloss: Write a proof so precisely that a computer can verify every step — turning mathematical certainty into a compile check.
concepts.formal_gloss: The translation of informal proofs into a formal logical calculus checked by software (proof assistants such as Lean, Coq, Isabelle), with libraries like mathlib formalizing large bodies of mathematics.

concepts.name: Ramsey Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: Complete disorder is impossible — any party of six people must contain three mutual friends or three mutual strangers.
concepts.formal_gloss: For all k, l there exists R(k,l) such that every 2-coloring of the edges of a complete graph on R(k,l) vertices contains a monochromatic K_k or K_l; generalizations pervade combinatorics.

resources.title: Graham, Rothschild & Spencer, Ramsey Theory, 2nd ed. (Wiley)

sources: R(5,5) still unknown — live research edge

concepts.name: Random Graphs
concepts.intuition_gloss: Flip a coin for every possible wire in a network and watch structure crystallize — a giant connected blob suddenly emerges at a sharp threshold.
concepts.formal_gloss: The G(n,p) model includes each of the C(n,2) edges independently with probability p; properties like connectivity appear at sharp thresholds, e.g. the giant component emerges at p ≈ 1/n.

resources.title: Bollobás, Random Graphs, 2nd ed. (Cambridge University Press)

sources: foundation of modern network science (territory tagline: 'networks everywhere')

concepts.name: Rank–Nullity Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: A conservation law for dimensions: the directions a map crushes plus the directions that survive in its output always add back up to the space you started with.
concepts.formal_gloss: For a linear map T on a finite-dimensional space V, dim ker T + dim im T = dim V.

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 3

sources: Axler LADR ch. 3 ('fundamental theorem of linear maps')

concepts.name: Rational Expressions & Functions
concepts.intuition_gloss: A fraction built out of polynomials — it behaves like ordinary fraction arithmetic until the bottom hits zero, where the graph tears open into holes and asymptotes.
concepts.formal_gloss: A quotient p(x)/q(x) of polynomials with q not the zero polynomial, defined wherever q(x) ≠ 0; added, multiplied, and simplified by the rules of fraction arithmetic, with zeros of q producing vertical asymptotes or removable holes.

concepts.name: Reasoning by Analogy
concepts.intuition_gloss: Borrow the shape of a solved problem to attack an unsolved one — spheres are to space what circles are to the plane.
concepts.formal_gloss: The heuristic of transferring conjectures, methods, or structures between domains that share partial structural correspondence, generating plausible but unproven claims to be verified.
sources: Polya, Mathematics and Plausible Reasoning vol. 1 (Induction and Analogy in Mathematics)

concepts.name: Riemann Zeta Function & the Riemann Hypothesis
concepts.intuition_gloss: A single smooth function whose hidden zeros conduct the rhythm of the primes — and the million-dollar conjecture that they all line up on one vertical line.
concepts.formal_gloss: ζ(s) = Σ n^(-s) = Π_p (1 - p^(-s))^(-1) for Re(s) > 1, continued analytically to C \ {1}; the Riemann hypothesis asserts all nontrivial zeros have real part 1/2.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Visualizing the Riemann zeta function and analytic continuation
LMFDB — L-functions and Modular Forms Database

sources: Princeton Companion IV.2 & V (RH as open problem)

concepts.name: Riemannian Geometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: Geometry from the inside: give every point of a space its own tiny ruler, and distance, angle, and curvature all follow — the mathematics of curved universes.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of smooth manifolds equipped with a Riemannian metric — a smoothly varying inner product on tangent spaces — yielding lengths, geodesics, and curvature tensors.
sources: Princeton Companion (Riemannian geometry / Ricci flow chapters)
modern leverage: general relativity, Perelman's proof of the Poincaré conjecture

concepts.name: Rigor & Intuition
concepts.intuition_gloss: Mathematicians run on fast intuition checked by slow rigor — mastery is when the two agree, and your gut instantly senses what a careful proof would confirm.
concepts.formal_gloss: The interplay between informal heuristic understanding and formal deductive verification, often described as Tao's three stages: pre-rigorous, rigorous, and post-rigorous practice.

resources.title: There's more to mathematics than rigour and proofs — Terence Tao
On Proof and Progress in Mathematics — W. Thurston (arXiv)

sources: Thurston, On Proof and Progress in Mathematics (1994)

concepts.name: Root-Finding & Newton's Method
concepts.intuition_gloss: To solve an equation numerically, either trap the answer in a bracket and keep halving it, or slide down the tangent line to a better guess — Newton's tangent trick roughly doubles your correct digits at each step near the answer.
concepts.formal_gloss: Iterative schemes for solving f(x) = 0: bisection (halving a sign-change bracket, gaining one bit per step), fixed-point iteration, and canonically Newton's iteration x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n), which converges quadratically near a simple root.
sources: standard numerical analysis first courses (Burden & Faires ch. 2)
Süli & Mayers, An Introduction to Numerical Analysis ch. 1

concepts.name: RSA & Number-Theoretic Cryptography
concepts.intuition_gloss: Euler's 250-year-old clock theorem, run in reverse with a secret key, lets strangers exchange locked messages over open channels.
concepts.formal_gloss: RSA encrypts by c = m^e mod n for n = pq and decrypts by m = c^d mod n where ed ≡ 1 (mod φ(n)); security rests on the hardness of factoring n, and Diffie-Hellman analogously on discrete logarithms.
sources: the standard 'why number theory matters' payoff in modern curricula

concepts.name: Set Partitions, Stirling & Bell Numbers
aka: Stirling numbers of the second kind
concepts.intuition_gloss: Count the ways to split a group of people into unlabeled teams — Stirling numbers count the splits into exactly k teams, Bell numbers count all possible splits at once.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set partition of an n-set is a family of disjoint nonempty blocks covering it (equivalently, an equivalence relation); S(n,k) counts partitions into k blocks via S(n,k) = k*S(n-1,k) + S(n-1,k-1), the Bell number B_n = sum over k of S(n,k), and the twelvefold way organizes balls-in-boxes counting by labeled/unlabeled and injective/surjective constraints.

resources.title: Stirling numbers of the second kind — Wikipedia

sources: companion to integer partitions: partitioning a set rather than a number

concepts.name: Simple Groups & the Classification
concepts.intuition_gloss: The unsplittable atoms of symmetry — and the century-long census that found them all, ending in eighteen orderly families plus twenty-six bizarre outliers crowned by the Monster.
concepts.formal_gloss: A group whose only normal subgroups are trivial; the classification theorem states every finite simple group is cyclic of prime order, alternating, of Lie type, or one of 26 sporadic groups.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Group theory, abstraction, and the 196,883-dimensional monster
Wikipedia: Classification of finite simple groups

sources: Princeton Companion V.7 (Classification of Finite Simple Groups)

concepts.name: Stochastic Differential Equation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A rule of change with random jitter built in — the path of a pollen grain buffeted by molecules, or a stock price wandering upward.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equation dX_t = μ(X_t)dt + σ(X_t)dW_t driven by Brownian motion, given rigorous meaning by the Itô (or Stratonovich) stochastic integral.

concepts.name: Vector Calculus & Stokes' Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: What flows out through a boundary equals what is generated inside — the fundamental theorem of calculus scaled up to curves, surfaces, and volumes.
concepts.formal_gloss: ∫_∂M ω = ∫_M dω: the integral of a differential form over the boundary of an oriented manifold equals the integral of its exterior derivative over the interior, unifying Green's, divergence, and classical Stokes theorems.

concepts.name: Strong Induction & Induction Variants
concepts.intuition_gloss: Ordinary induction climbs a ladder one rung at a time; strong induction lets you stand on every rung below you at once — assume the claim for all smaller cases, not just the previous one.
concepts.formal_gloss: Variants of the induction principle: complete (strong) induction assumes the statement for all values below n to prove it at n; well-ordering arguments take a minimal counterexample and derive a contradiction; structural induction extends the scheme to recursively defined objects.

concepts.name: Taylor Series
concepts.intuition_gloss: Cloning a function near a point using only its derivatives there — better and better polynomial impressions of the real thing.
concepts.formal_gloss: The series Σ f^(n)(a)(x-a)^n / n!, which represents f near a when the remainder term tends to zero.

concepts.name: Tensors & Multilinear Algebra
concepts.intuition_gloss: The machinery for quantities that respond linearly to several inputs at once — matrices generalized to grids of any depth.
concepts.formal_gloss: Elements of tensor product spaces V ⊗ W, equivalently multilinear maps; type-(p,q) tensors transform predictably under change of basis.
sources: standard multilinear algebra chapters (Roman, Advanced Linear Algebra)

concepts.name: Tessellation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Covering an endless floor with repeating tiles and no gaps — the geometry of wallpaper, honeycombs, and Escher prints.
concepts.formal_gloss: A covering of the plane by non-overlapping tiles; periodic tilings are classified by the 17 wallpaper groups, while aperiodic tile sets (Penrose) tile only without repetition.
sources: recreational-to-research pipeline: Penrose tilings, 2023 aperiodic monotile

concepts.name: Trace
concepts.intuition_gloss: Add up a matrix's diagonal and you get a number that doesn't care which coordinates you drew — the combined total of its stretch factors.
concepts.formal_gloss: The sum of the diagonal entries of a square matrix; basis-invariant (tr(P⁻¹AP) = tr A), satisfies tr(AB) = tr(BA), and equals the sum of the eigenvalues counted with algebraic multiplicity.

resources.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, ch. 8

concepts.name: Trees & Spanning Trees
concepts.intuition_gloss: A network with no loops — the leanest possible way to connect everything, from family trees to file systems.
concepts.formal_gloss: A connected acyclic graph on n vertices, equivalently a graph with exactly n-1 edges and a unique path between every pair; Cayley's formula counts n^(n-2) labeled trees on n vertices.
sources: ubiquitous data structure — high leverage into computation

concepts.name: Trigonometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: Ratios in right triangles become functions that encode every rotation and every repeating wave.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of sine, cosine, and tangent, defined on the unit circle as coordinates of a rotated point, satisfying identities such as sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.
sources: leverage: gateway to Fourier analysis, complex exponentials, and all wave phenomena

concepts.name: Type Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: An alternative bedrock where every object carries a type like a units label, and a proof literally is a program — the foundation running inside Lean and Coq.
concepts.formal_gloss: A foundational framework of typed terms and dependent types in which propositions are types and proofs are their inhabitants, underlying modern proof assistants.

resources.title: Type Theory — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy

concepts.name: Uniform Continuity
concepts.intuition_gloss: One wiggle allowance for the whole domain: pick your output tolerance and a single input allowance works everywhere at once, not a different one at each point.
concepts.formal_gloss: f is uniformly continuous if for every ε > 0 there is δ > 0 with |f(x) - f(y)| < ε whenever |x - y| < δ, for all x, y in the domain; continuous functions on compact sets are uniformly continuous (Heine–Cantor), which is why they are Riemann integrable.

concepts.name: Factorization in Rings (Euclidean, PID, UFD)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Asking which number-worlds keep the 'every number breaks uniquely into primes' promise — and meeting the famous worlds where the promise fails.
concepts.formal_gloss: The hierarchy Euclidean domain ⊂ principal ideal domain ⊂ unique factorization domain ⊂ integral domain, classifying rings by whether division, single-generator ideals, and unique irreducible factorization survive.

concepts.name: Vector Field
concepts.intuition_gloss: Attach an arrow to every point of space — like wind on a weather map — and you have a complete picture of motion everywhere at once.
concepts.formal_gloss: An assignment x ↦ v(x) of a vector to each point of a region (or manifold); smooth vector fields are the right-hand sides of autonomous ODEs x' = v(x) and generate flows by integration.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Divergence and curl: The language of Maxwell's equations, fluid flow, and more

sources: Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (vector fields on the line and plane)

concepts.name: Wave Equation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Pluck a string and the disturbance travels: its shape persists while its position moves, at a speed set by the medium.
concepts.formal_gloss: The hyperbolic PDE u_tt = c²∇²u, with traveling-wave solutions f(x − ct) + g(x + ct) by d'Alembert's formula and standing-wave normal modes on bounded domains.

concepts.name: Wilson's Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Multiply together every hour on a prime-sized clock and you always land exactly one step before the top — and that only happens when the clock size is prime.
concepts.formal_gloss: (n-1)! ≡ -1 (mod n) if and only if n is prime; a complete characterization of primality, though useless as a practical test since factorials grow too fast.

concepts.name: Winding Number & Degree
concepts.intuition_gloss: Walk a loop around the plane while watching a fixed point: the winding number counts how many net times you circle it — and no continuous wiggling of the loop can change that whole number.
concepts.formal_gloss: For a closed curve in the plane avoiding a point p, the integer number of counterclockwise turns the curve makes around p; it is invariant under homotopy avoiding p, computes π1 of the punctured plane and the circle (both Z), and generalizes to the degree of a map between spheres.
sources: standard payoff of π1(S¹) ≅ Z in Hatcher §1.1 and Munkres Ch. 9
argument principle / residue connection in complex analysis curricula

concepts.name: Without Loss of Generality
concepts.intuition_gloss: When several scenarios are mirror images of each other, prove just one and let symmetry stamp the result onto the rest — this is the proof-writing move, while exploiting symmetry is the wider discovery strategy behind it.
concepts.formal_gloss: A proof step restricting attention to one case among several that are equivalent under a symmetry of the problem, the other cases following by the same argument mutatis mutandis.
sources: standard proof-writing conventions (Velleman, Book of Proof)

concepts.name: Working Backwards
concepts.intuition_gloss: Stand at the finish line and ask 'what would get me here?', walking the maze in reverse until you meet the entrance.
concepts.formal_gloss: The heuristic of reasoning from the desired conclusion to sufficient intermediate conditions until reaching the given hypotheses, then presenting the argument in forward order.
sources: Polya, How to Solve It (working backwards / Pappus's analysis)
